An extended period of dry weather continued to worsen across portions of Central and South Central Kansas during the month of May, as some areas saw little or no appreciable amounts of rainfall.  Areas north of Wichita dried out even further during the middle portion of the month, pushing areas in Central Kansas, into the severe drought category (D2).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A drought began to worsen across Central Kansas during the middle portion of the month.  The drought reached severe levels (D2)across the area during the week of May 10th and lasted until May 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms affected south-central Kansas during the mid-evening hours on the 8th, producing hail up to ping-pong ball size in Reno county. As the storms collapsed during the late evening hours on the 8th through the early morning hours on the 9th, very strong winds up to 70 mph were recorded across the Wichita area due to evaporative cooling effects from the collapsing storms. As a side note, environmental conditions were just right to produce a relatively rare heat burst across the Wichita area. Temperatures quickly rose from 85 to around 102 degrees.

A wind gust to 62 mph was measured at McConnell Airforce Base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe winter storm brought near blizzard and blizzard conditions to much of eastern and northeastern Kansas on February 1 2011.  Many areas received 6 to 10 inches of wind blown snow within a 12 hour period.  Most of the snow fell between 6am and 9pm on February 1.  However on the previous day the entire area endured a daylong light freezing rain and freezing drizzle which accumulated to around one tenth of an inch in most spots.  So the combination of the ice beneath heavy blowing snow brought dangerous driving conditions to the area.  In addition, air temperatures hovered in the single digits and teens which when combined with winds produced wind chill indices of 10 to 20 below during the and after the snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported 6 to 10 inches of snow which had accumulated to 6 inches by around noon on February 1. Significant blowing and drifting also occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow began to fall shortly after sunrise across parts of North Central Kansas and gradually spread southeast across all of the region.  The heaviest snow fell along the I-70 corridor from Manhattan east through Topeka and Lawrence and points north and east from there.  Many areas received 5 to 8 inches from this storm and the snow accumulated to 6 inches or more within 12 hours of onset in several counties.  The snow was over for the most part by midnight Thursday, January 20th or just 12 hours after it began in many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS measured 8 inches of snow within 12 hours of onset.  This was on the high end of the reported range across the area with most spots reporting around 6 inches.

An extended period of dry weather continued to worsen across portions of Central and South Central Kansas during the month of June,  as some areas saw little or no appreciable rainfall.  Areas south and west of Wichita dried out even further during the second half of the month, pushing this area into extreme drought (D3). Other areas in Central Kansas, continued to be in a severe drought (D2).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3), continued across South Central Kansas during the month of June.  This was a continuation of a drought that started in late April and continued through the month of May and into June. The wheat harvest was worst seen in over 15 years, with some farmers abandoning as much as half of their wheat crop and try and plant other crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe winter storm brought near blizzard and blizzard conditions to much of eastern and northeastern Kansas on February 1 2011.  Many areas received 6 to 10 inches of wind blown snow within a 12 hour period.  Most of the snow fell between 6am and 9pm on February 1.  However on the previous day the entire area endured a daylong light freezing rain and freezing drizzle which accumulated to around one tenth of an inch in most spots.  So the combination of the ice beneath heavy blowing snow brought dangerous driving conditions to the area.  In addition, air temperatures hovered in the single digits and teens which when combined with winds produced wind chill indices of 10 to 20 below during the and after the snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported 6 to 10 inches of snow which had accumulated to 6 inches by around 4pm on February 1. Significant blowing and drifting also occurred. Near blizzard to blizzard conditions also occurred during the afternoon of February 1.

An unusually early season heat wave moved into Central and South Central Kansas on May 9th, 2011.  This very warm airmass led to record high temperatures falling across the region with Russell, Salina and Wichita, Kansas all setting record high temperatures.  This early season heat left some people underprepared for the stifling hear with one heat related death.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in Wichita, Kansas set a record high temperature of 100 degrees.  With the temperature reaching the century mark, this is the earliest in the year that Wichita has ever recorded a 100 degree temperature.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures near 100 degrees led to the death of a Hispanic man in Rose Hill, Kansas. The 30 year old male collapsed while working on a roof and later died from heat stroke.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A drought began to worsen across South Central Kansas during the middle portion of the month.  The drought reached severe levels (D2) across the area during the week of May 10th and lasted until May 24th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A drought began to worsen across Central Kansas during the middle portion of the month.  The drought reached severe levels (D2) across the area during the week of May 10th and lasted until May 24th.

A severe winter storm brought near blizzard and blizzard conditions to much of eastern and northeastern Kansas on February 1 2011.  Many areas received 6 to 10 inches of wind blown snow within a 12 hour period.  Most of the snow fell between 6am and 9pm on February 1.  However on the previous day the entire area endured a daylong light freezing rain and freezing drizzle which accumulated to around one tenth of an inch in most spots.  So the combination of the ice beneath heavy blowing snow brought dangerous driving conditions to the area.  In addition, air temperatures hovered in the single digits and teens which when combined with winds produced wind chill indices of 10 to 20 below during the and after the snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported 6 inches of snow which had accumulated by around 4pm on February 1. Significant blowing and drifting also occurred. This was in addition to the light ice accumulation that occurred during the day of Jan 31st.

An extended period of dry weather continued to worsen across portions of Central and South Central Kansas during the month of June,  as some areas saw little or no appreciable rainfall.  Areas south and west of Wichita dried out even further during the second half of the month, pushing this area into extreme drought (D3). Other areas in Central Kansas, continued to be in a severe drought (D2).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought (D2), continued across Central Kansas during the month of June.  This was a continuation of a drought that started in late April and continued through the month of May and into June. The wheat harvest was the worst seen in over 15 years, with some farmers abandoning as much as half of their wheat crop and try and plant other crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy banded snow developed during the pre-dawn hours of Tuesday February 8th and continued through the day and into the overnight hours ending early on February 9th. A narrow band of very heavy snow fell across portions of Ottawa, Dickinson, Morris, Lyon and into Coffey and Anderson Counties where amounts of 6 to 12 inches were common. A few spots reported amounts in excess of 12 inches however those were isolated cases. The snow was accompanied by air temperatures of less than 10 degrees and wind chills of zero to 10 below.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reports from sources including law enforcement estimated between 6 and 9 inches of snow fell across central and southern Dickinson County during this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day on Sunday, temperatures warmed to record highs across northeast Kansas before a powerful storm system moved through Sunday afternoon and evening.  Several locations reported hail up to 2 inches in diameter and winds of 70-80 mph with a few reports of structural damage due to straight line winds. Thunderstorms initiated late in the day behind a sharp cold front and quickly went severe. A well formed gustnado developed along the frontal boundary and rear flank boundary intersection near Williamstown on Highway 24. NWS survey crew identified areas of enhanced wind damage from a well formed gustnado in Williamstown. Two center pivot irrigations were flipped over, a large outdoor shed was destroyed and several tree limbs were snapped.

A powerful storm brought a bout of severe weather to Kansas on April 14th.  Severe thunderstorms including supercell storms developed ahead of a dryline and cold front during the afternoon.  Moisture was a limiting factor in the coverage and overall intensity of the storms however there was at least one brief tornado and several other funnel clouds that were documented across north central Kansas during the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high precipitation supercell initially developed to the north of Greensburg, Kansas and Pratt, Kansas, where an existing outflow boundary intersected the main synoptic cold front.  The supercells quickly dropped southeast into Barber and Harper County, Kansas. These intense supercell thunderstorms evolved into a bow echo type storm as it moved into Barber and Harper Counties, producing widespread wind damage across Harper County, Kansas.  Additional thunderstorms developed along the main cold frontal boundary across Central Kansas and produced damaging winds across portions of McPherson County near Goessel and Harvey County near Hesston and Newton.  The line of storms continued to drop southeast across much of Southern Kansas producing much needed rainfall to most of the area.

A powerful storm brought a bout of severe weather to Kansas on April 14th.  Severe thunderstorms including supercell storms developed ahead of a dryline and cold front during the afternoon.  Moisture was a limiting factor in the coverage and overall intensity of the storms however there was at least one brief tornado and several other funnel clouds that were documented across north central Kansas during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief rope tornado was witnessed by the public who also attempted to catch the tornado with a photograph.  The photo is looking east as a rain curtain wraps around the funnel and makes the funnel difficult to see.  The witness stated that the tornado as described as a rope was only on the ground for a moment before lifting. No damage occurred.

Warm temperatures returned to portions of northeast Kansas on August 23rd. Overnight lows on the 24th remained in the mid 70s.  August 24th brought warm temperatures again, as heat indices topped out around 112 degrees across areas along and south of Interstate 70.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures crept over 100 degrees in late August as another warm front pushed northward. The frontal boundary did not make it much further north than Interstate 70 before it stalled.  Heat indices across the area reached to between 108 and 112 degrees as the warm air mass carried quite a bit of moisture with it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cool front slid into the area and thunderstorms developed along this front.  A few of the storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage was done to a metal outbuilding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day on Sunday, temperatures warmed to record highs across northeast Kansas before a powerful storm system moved through Sunday afternoon and evening.  Several locations reported hail up to 2 inches in diameter and winds of 70-80 mph with a few reports of structural damage due to straight line winds. Thunderstorms initiated late in the day behind a sharp cold front and quickly went severe. A well formed gustnado developed along the frontal boundary and rear flank boundary intersection near Williamstown on Highway 24. NWS survey crew identified areas of enhanced wind damage from a well formed gustnado in Williamstown. Two center pivot irrigations were flipped over, a large outdoor shed was destroyed and several tree limbs were snapped.

A powerful storm brought a bout of severe weather to Kansas on April 14th.  Severe thunderstorms including supercell storms developed ahead of a dryline and cold front during the afternoon.  Moisture was a limiting factor in the coverage and overall intensity of the storms however there was at least one brief tornado and several other funnel clouds that were documented across north central Kansas during the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures returned to portions of northeast Kansas on August 23rd. Overnight lows on the 24th remained in the mid 70s.  August 24th brought warm temperatures again, as heat indices topped out around 112 degrees across areas along and south of Interstate 70.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures crept over 100 degrees in late August as another warm front pushed northward. The frontal boundary did not make it much further north than Interstate 70 before it stalled.  Heat indices across the area reached to between 108 and 112 degrees as the warm air mass carried quite a bit of moisture with it.

Heat indices across the area reached to between 108 and 112 degrees as the warm air mass carried quite a bit of moisture with it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of August 19, a line of very strong thunderstorms including a couple embedded supercells moved southward out of Nebraska and into northeast Kansas. The storms left behind an expansive swath of structure and crop damage as the storms produced very large hail and 90 to 100 mph winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of wind damage east of Whiting from the Public and Trained Spotters. Reports of several outbuildings destroyed, power poles and highway signs blown over, wind gust estimated greater than 90 mph. Four broken windows in home, skylight broken in outdoor building. Corn fields stripped, 12 inch diameter tree limbs down in yard. Siding damaged by wind blown hail, three foot diameter trees uprooted, a total of eight trees down on property. Report from spotter of 6 to 12 inch diameter trees limbs down east of Whiting. Three windows broken in home from wind blown hail. One foot diameter tree limbs down with a large outdoor shed destroyed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager estimates winds of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the overnight hours on August 18, a line of strong thunderstorms moved out of southeast Nebraska into northeast Kansas. These storms brought periods of strong winds of 70 to 80 mph, with some locations reporting damage to trees and structures.

A high precipitation supercell initially developed to the north of Greensburg, Kansas and Pratt, Kansas, where an existing outflow boundary intersected the main synoptic cold front.  The supercells quickly dropped southeast into Barber and Harper County, Kansas. These intense supercell thunderstorms evolved into a bow echo type storm as it moved into Barber and Harper Counties, producing widespread wind damage across Harper County, Kansas.  Additional thunderstorms developed along the main cold frontal boundary across Central Kansas and produced damaging winds across portions of McPherson County near Goessel and Harvey County near Hesston and Newton.  The line of storms continued to drop southeast across much of Southern Kansas producing much needed rainfall to most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor street flooding was reported in the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell thunderstorm moved from Imperial, Nebraska southeast to Benkelman, and traveled across northwest Kansas through Bird City, McDonald, to Colby and Oakley before weakening across Gove county Kansas.  Widespread wind and hail damage was reported with the storm through the late afternoon hours.  A second strong thunderstorm moved southeast during the evening, producing large hail over Hitchcock county Nebraska and Norton county Kansas.

A series of supercell thunderstorms moved southeast during the afternoon and evening hours, resulting in significant wind and hail damage.  Hail up to the size of baseballs and wind gusts to 70 mph produced damage to vehicles, crops and buildings in the area.

During the day on Sunday, temperatures warmed to record highs across northeast Kansas before a powerful storm system moved through Sunday afternoon and evening.  Several locations reported hail up to 2 inches in diameter and winds of 70-80 mph with a few reports of structural damage due to straight line winds. Thunderstorms initiated late in the day behind a sharp cold front and quickly went severe. A well formed gustnado developed along the frontal boundary and rear flank boundary intersection near Williamstown on Highway 24. NWS survey crew identified areas of enhanced wind damage from a well formed gustnado in Williamstown. Two center pivot irrigations were flipped over, a large outdoor shed was destroyed and several tree limbs were snapped.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter also reported minor tree and leaf damage with the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of strong to marginally severe thunderstorms moved northeast across southeast Kansas during the afternoon of the 21st, ahead of an upper level disturbance approaching from the west. Along with localized areas of heavy rain across southeast Kansas, dime size hail was reported in Coffeyville.

An extended period of dry weather began to worsen across portions of Southwest and South Central Kansas during the second half of April.   Areas south and west of Wichita began to creep into the the severe drought category (D2).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A drought began to worsen across South Central Kansas during the last part of the month.  The drought reached D2 levels across the area during the week of April 26th and lasted through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow from thunderstorms earlier in the day over Nebraska helped trigger new storms during the afternoon over Rawlins county. As the afternoon progressed, storms increased in coverage, and more storms moved into the region from eastern Colorado. Most reports from the public and spotters were for heavy rainfall and isolated hail, but one heavy rain event led to damage as a police car had to be abandoned due to high water in Colby.

The supercells quickly dropped southeast into Barber and Harper County, Kansas. These intense supercell thunderstorms evolved into a bow echo type storm as it moved into Barber and Harper Counties, producing widespread wind damage across Harper County, Kansas.  Additional thunderstorms developed along the main cold frontal boundary across Central Kansas and produced damaging winds across portions of McPherson County near Goessel and Harvey County near Hesston and Newton.  The line of storms continued to drop southeast across much of Southern Kansas producing much needed rainfall to most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powerlines were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 5, scattered thunderstorms formed in the early morning hours. Damaging winds up to 60 mph were reported across portions of north central Kansas. Minor damage, consisting of broken tree limbs and downed power lines, was reported across the areas with the strongest winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Manhattan, tree limbs snapped causing transformers to explode and fail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stormy conditions brought strong winds and heavy rain to portions of northeast Kansas on August 7. Only a few reports of marginally severe hail came to the office, however some areas experienced some heavy damage, including large diameter trees snapped, outbuildings destroyed, and moderate flooding in city streets, as a result of the strong winds and heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: More than 2.5 inches of rain fell across Geary County. As a result portions of Junction City flooded, namely at the 700 block of 14th Street, and at 14th and Clay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day on Sunday, temperatures warmed to record highs across northeast Kansas before a powerful storm system moved through Sunday afternoon and evening.  Several locations reported hail up to 2 inches in diameter and winds of 70-80 mph with a few reports of structural damage due to straight line winds. Thunderstorms initiated late in the day behind a sharp cold front and quickly went severe. A well formed gustnado developed along the frontal boundary and rear flank boundary intersection near Williamstown on Highway 24. NWS survey crew identified areas of enhanced wind damage from a well formed gustnado in Williamstown. Two center pivot irrigations were flipped over, a large outdoor shed was destroyed and several tree limbs were snapped.

Dry conditions began to impact portions of extreme southeastern Kansas in July as D0 drought developed over the state and spread north and east into the region at the start of the month. By the end of July, D2 drought had advanced into extreme southeastern portions of the Kansas as rainfall had become sparse.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southeastern portion of Kansas saw continuing deterioration through the month as corn, soybean and other crops were in very poor condition across the area. A few complete crop failures were reported along with burned up pastures which forced livestock producers to feed hay in some areas. In addition, the diminishing pastures and water supplies for cattle, forced many to sell off or cull herds. Less than two inches of rainfall were typical over the region. Precipitation amounts across the area were between a quarter and half of the normal rainfall for the month. This drought began in July and was ongoing through the month of September. The loss and damage estimates will be included in the August storm data section for the entire drought event.

A high precipitation supercell initially developed to the north of Greensburg, Kansas and Pratt, Kansas, where an existing outflow boundary intersected the main synoptic cold front.  The supercells quickly dropped southeast into Barber and Harper County, Kansas. These intense supercell thunderstorms evolved into a bow echo type storm as it moved into Barber and Harper Counties, producing widespread wind damage across Harper County, Kansas.  Additional thunderstorms developed along the main cold frontal boundary across Central Kansas and produced damaging winds across portions of McPherson County near Goessel and Harvey County near Hesston and Newton.  The line of storms continued to drop southeast across much of Southern Kansas producing much needed rainfall to most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread tree damage and a few downed powerlines were noted as the storms moved through.

During the day on Sunday, temperatures warmed to record highs across northeast Kansas before a powerful storm system moved through Sunday afternoon and evening.  Several locations reported hail up to 2 inches in diameter and winds of 70-80 mph with a few reports of structural damage due to straight line winds. Thunderstorms initiated late in the day behind a sharp cold front and quickly went severe. A well formed gustnado developed along the frontal boundary and rear flank boundary intersection near Williamstown on Highway 24. NWS survey crew identified areas of enhanced wind damage from a well formed gustnado in Williamstown. Two center pivot irrigations were flipped over, a large outdoor shed was destroyed and several tree limbs were snapped.

Winds of 70 to 80 mph accompanied the storms producing damage to trees and utility poles.  In addition, large hail and several funnel cloud reports were also received.

During the day on Sunday, temperatures warmed to record highs across northeast Kansas before a powerful storm system moved through Sunday afternoon and evening.  Several locations reported hail up to 2 inches in diameter and winds of 70-80 mph with a few reports of structural damage due to straight line winds. Thunderstorms initiated late in the day behind a sharp cold front and quickly went severe. A well formed gustnado developed along the frontal boundary and rear flank boundary intersection near Williamstown on Highway 24. NWS survey crew identified areas of enhanced wind damage from a well formed gustnado in Williamstown. Two center pivot irrigations were flipped over, a large outdoor shed was destroyed and several tree limbs were snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high precipitation supercell initially developed to the north of Greensburg, Kansas and Pratt, Kansas, where an existing outflow boundary intersected the main synoptic cold front.  The supercells quickly dropped southeast into Barber and Harper County, Kansas. These intense supercell thunderstorms evolved into a bow echo type storm as it moved into Barber and Harper Counties, producing widespread wind damage across Harper County, Kansas.  Additional thunderstorms developed along the main cold frontal boundary across Central Kansas and produced damaging winds across portions of McPherson County near Goessel and Harvey County near Hesston and Newton.  The line of storms continued to drop southeast across much of Southern Kansas producing much needed rainfall to most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell thunderstorm moved from Imperial, Nebraska southeast to Benkelman, and traveled across northwest Kansas through Bird City, McDonald, to Colby and Oakley before weakening across Gove county Kansas.  Widespread wind and hail damage was reported with the storm through the late afternoon hours.  A second strong thunderstorm moved southeast during the evening, producing large hail over Hitchcock county Nebraska and Norton county Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gusts of over 70 MPH reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stormy conditions brought strong winds and heavy rain to portions of northeast Kansas on August 7. Only a few reports of marginally severe hail came to the office, however some areas experienced some heavy damage, including large diameter trees snapped, outbuildings destroyed, and moderate flooding in city streets, as a result of the strong winds and heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was knocked over 2 miles south of Alta Vista.

An extended period of dry weather eased some across portions of Central and South Central Kansas during the month of November 2011. Some welcome rainfall, in the form of thunderstorms, did fall across most of the region during the beginning of the month. This brought some drought relief, with most areas seeing their first above normal rainfall in almost a year.  More welcome rainfall occurred at the end of the month as well.  Portions of South Central and Southern Kansas saw their first drought improvement in almost six months, as some areas southwest of Wichita went from an exceptional drought (D4) to an extreme drought (D3).  Other areas of southern Kansas improved from an extreme drought (D3) to a severe drought (D2).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) continued across Southeast Kansas during the first part of the month, but rainfall early in the month and late in the month actually improved the drought conditions by the end of November. The drought remained at extreme levels (D3) across the area through November 22nd, but eased to an severe drought (D2) by the end of the month. This was first improvement in the drought conditions since the drought began in late April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow began to fall shortly after sunrise across parts of North Central Kansas and gradually spread southeast across all of the region.  The heaviest snow fell along the I-70 corridor from Manhattan east through Topeka and Lawrence and points north and east from there.  Many areas received 5 to 8 inches from this storm and the snow accumulated to 6 inches or more within 12 hours of onset in several counties.  The snow was over for the most part by midnight Thursday, January 20th or just 12 hours after it began in many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southern half of Brown and Nemaha Counties also received 6 inches of snow in less than 12 hours during this event.  In Brown County law enforcement estimated 6 inches of snow in Hiawatha from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged snow event began during the pre-dawn hours of Monday January 10th across Northeast Kansas.  Snow fell during the early morning hours and accumulated in many areas to between 4 and 6 inches.  The snow stopped for a period of several hours during the late morning and early afternoon hours before re-developing by 3pm local time. Another 1 to 3 inches of accumulation then occurred through 11 pm Monday January 10th.  All told there were several counties that did receive 6 to 9 inches of snowfall in a period of 18 to 24 hours.  Those counties have been noted in stormdata.  We have included a snowfall map however it must be noted that 1-3 inches of snow did fall the previous day across the counties bordering Nebraska which has inflated the totals on this map.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shawnee County received anywhere from 6 to 9 inches of snowfall during a 18 to 24 hour period ending just before midnight on January 10th.  The NWS office in Topeka measured over 9 inches of snow during this period of time.  The winds were light during the snowfall event and the intensity was not heavy therefore the impacts were mitigated to an extent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure moved across Northwest Kansas bringing snowfall amounts of six to almost eleven inches with it. The snowfall began during the early morning, then began to taper off during the afternoon and evening hours. The highest snowfall amounts were observed along a line from near Goodland where 6.0 inches were reported, northeast to Herndon where 12.0 inches were reported.  I-70 from Colby to the Colorado border was closed through the evening due to snow packed roads causing multiple accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured snowfall amount of 7.0 inches was reported in Levant.  The Colby Police Department dealt with about a half dozen slide offs in town.  Thomas County Sheriff's department assisted with numerous slide-offs on I-70, and an accident on K-25.  During the night a semi was stuck on County Road 2 near Brewster, blocking the entire road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 27, 2011 severe storms moved through portions of northeast Kansas bringing heavy rain and marginally severe hail.
A stationary frontal boundary was located across east central Kansas. Shortly lived thunderstorms popped up along the boundary and caused isolated cases of quarter sized hail and strong gusty winds.

On July 27, 28, and 29, multiple thunderstorms developed near a slowly moving cold front which spread across northeast Kansas. The very warm surface conditions led to an environment that was supportive of downbursts. A few of the thunderstorms quickly intensified, but with lack of any mid-level shear to support the updrafts; the storms quickly collapsed upon themselves, bringing strong downpours and damaging winds. The most intense of these downburst thunderstorms formed in Ottawa County and brought 70 to 80 mph winds to areas near Wells and Oak Hill.  Numerous outbuildings were destroyed and trees uprooted as a result of the damaging wind gusts. Areas of Riley and Pottawatomie Counties also recieved strong winds, and in Jefferson County, a boating dock broke loose from the Lake Perry Marina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter estimates winds of 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Outflow from a nearby thunderstorm produced 60 mph winds near Olsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 12, 2011 scattered thunderstorms produced very strong winds across portions of northeast Kansas. The worst damage took place across Riley and Pottawatomie Counties where power poles and large structures were destroyed in that area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A west-facing sliding glass patio door was broken.

Supercell storms developed in Ottawa and Cloud counties during the late afternoon and early evening hours of June 20, 2011. Hail up to the size of 2 inches in diameter was reported with these storms. As the storms moved east, they formed into a line of storms producing strong and damaging winds with numerous reports of 3-4 inch tree limbs down. The storms weakened as they moved east across Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of dry weather eased some across portions of Central and South Central Kansas during the month of November 2011. Some welcome rainfall, in the form of thunderstorms, did fall across most of the region during the beginning of the month. This brought some drought relief, with most areas seeing their first above normal rainfall in almost a year.  More welcome rainfall occurred at the end of the month as well.  Portions of South Central and Southern Kansas saw their first drought improvement in almost six months, as some areas southwest of Wichita went from an exceptional drought (D4) to an extreme drought (D3).  Other areas of southern Kansas improved from an extreme drought (D3) to a severe drought (D2).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional drought (D4) continued across South Central Kansas during the first part of the month, but rainfall early in the month and late in the month actually improved the drought conditions by the end of November. The drought remained at exceptional levels (D4) across the area through November 22nd, but eased to an extreme drought (D3) by the end of the month. This was first improvement in the drought conditions since the drought began in late April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged snow event began during the pre-dawn hours of Monday January 10th across Northeast Kansas.  Snow fell during the early morning hours and accumulated in many areas to between 4 and 6 inches.  The snow stopped for a period of several hours during the late morning and early afternoon hours before re-developing by 3pm local time. Another 1 to 3 inches of accumulation then occurred through 11 pm Monday January 10th.  All told there were several counties that did receive 6 to 9 inches of snowfall in a period of 18 to 24 hours.  Those counties have been noted in stormdata.  We have included a snowfall map however it must be noted that 1-3 inches of snow did fall the previous day across the counties bordering Nebraska which has inflated the totals on this map.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jackson County received anywhere from 6 to 9 inches of snowfall during a 18 to 24 hour period ending just before midnight on January 10th. Reports of of Jackson County indicated 7 to 9 inches of snow had accumulated during this time frame. The winds were light during the snowfall event and the intensity was not heavy therefore the impacts were mitigated to an extent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 27, 28, and 29, multiple thunderstorms developed near a slowly moving cold front which spread across northeast Kansas. The very warm surface conditions led to an environment that was supportive of downbursts. A few of the thunderstorms quickly intensified, but with lack of any mid-level shear to support the updrafts; the storms quickly collapsed upon themselves, bringing strong downpours and damaging winds. The most intense of these downburst thunderstorms formed in Ottawa County and brought 70 to 80 mph winds to areas near Wells and Oak Hill.  Numerous outbuildings were destroyed and trees uprooted as a result of the damaging wind gusts. Areas of Riley and Pottawatomie Counties also recieved strong winds, and in Jefferson County, a boating dock broke loose from the Lake Perry Marina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter estimated winds of 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of dry weather continued unabated across portions of Central and South Central Kansas during the month of September 2011, as most of the area saw below normal levels of precipitation and even saw a few more triple digit temperatures for the beginning of the month. Some rainfall did fall during the middle of the month which brought some relief, but overall rainfall was disappointing, with most areas seeing rainfall amounts two inches below normal. Areas south and west of Wichita continued to remain very dry, keeping this area in an exceptional drought (D4), while others areas across Central Kansas remain in an extreme drought (D3). Other areas across the Flint Hills continued with a severe drought (D2) throughout the month. This is a continuation of the widespread drought that has been on going across the Plains for the entire summer of 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought (D2), continued across South Central Kansas during the month of September. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late July. Local cattle farmers had sold most of their cattle due to poor pasture conditions with little or no hay to feed them.  Due to the high demand, hay prices almost doubled in the past 12 months. Local grain elevators took in only half as much grain as it did in a normal year, due to the drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) drought continued into the month of November; however, above normal rainfall during the month eased drought effects. Rainfall was near twice the normal for some locations and this helped mitigate the drought, with many locations falling out of Extreme drought (D3) by the 22nd of the month and finally below Severe (D2) status by the end of the month. With drought conditions falling below Severe (D2) status, this will bring to an end to the 2011 drought information provided in previous months.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S Drought Monitor along with local reports of improving conditions from Extreme Drought (D3) to Moderate (D1) by the close of the month across the county. Rainfall of nearly twice the monthly normal was recorded during the month which allowed drought conditions to ease. This closes the drought that began across the region in July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds behind a cold front moving southeast through eastern Colorado caused winds to gust over 70 MPH during the late morning and afternoon. The high winds blew dirt and sand, which reduced visibilities to a quarter mile at times and caused I-70 to be closed, knocked down power poles and trees, and took the roof off at least one home.  Some of the higher gusts that were reported were 78 MPH at 12 SE of Sharon Springs, 77 MPH at Goodland, 70 MPH at Hoxie, 68 MPH at Weskan, 67 MPH at Bird City, 66 MPH 4 NW of Marienthal, and 64 MPH 4 N of Atwood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at Weskan.  Relayed by the KSN-TV station. A wind gust of 53 kts (61 mph) was also reported at a mesonet site 2 S of Wallace.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds behind a cold front moving southeast through eastern Colorado caused winds to gust over 70 MPH during the late morning and afternoon. The high winds blew dirt and sand, which reduced visibilities to a quarter mile at times and caused I-70 to be closed, knocked down power poles and trees, and took the roof off at least one home.  Some of the higher gusts that were reported were 78 MPH at 12 SE of Sharon Springs, 77 MPH at Goodland, 70 MPH at Hoxie, 68 MPH at Weskan, 67 MPH at Bird City, 66 MPH 4 NW of Marienthal, and 64 MPH 4 N of Atwood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of 12 inch diameter tree limb down along with numerous metal storage buildings and fences damaged.  Numerous homes with shingles blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 27, 28, and 29, multiple thunderstorms developed near a slowly moving cold front which spread across northeast Kansas. The very warm surface conditions led to an environment that was supportive of downbursts. A few of the thunderstorms quickly intensified, but with lack of any mid-level shear to support the updrafts; the storms quickly collapsed upon themselves, bringing strong downpours and damaging winds. The most intense of these downburst thunderstorms formed in Ottawa County and brought 70 to 80 mph winds to areas near Wells and Oak Hill.  Numerous outbuildings were destroyed and trees uprooted as a result of the damaging wind gusts. Areas of Riley and Pottawatomie Counties also recieved strong winds, and in Jefferson County, a boating dock broke loose from the Lake Perry Marina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report of pieces of siding and roofing being blown off. A small shed was ripped apart and pieces of it were thrown. A 12 foot trailer was thrown approx. 350 feet. Minor tree damage also reported.

Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) drought continued into the month of November; however, above normal rainfall during the month eased drought effects. Rainfall was near twice the normal for some locations and this helped mitigate the drought, with many locations falling out of Extreme drought (D3) by the 22nd of the month and finally below Severe (D2) status by the end of the month. With drought conditions falling below Severe (D2) status, this will bring to an end to the 2011 drought information provided in previous months.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S Drought Monitor along with local reports of improving conditions from Extreme Drought (D3) to Moderate (D1) by the close of the month across the county. Rainfall of nearly twice the monthly normal was recorded during the month which allowed drought conditions to ease. This closes the drought that began across the region in July.

The Saturday of Thanksgiving weekend was one of the overall windiest days of fall 2011 across this portion of North Central Kansas, especially for areas near and west of Highway 281, where there were several hours of sustained north-northwest winds around 40 MPH accompanied by gusts around 50 MPH.||These winds occurred in the wake of a strong upper level low pressure system and associated powerful surface cold front, and were driven by the combination of a tight surface pressure gradient and deep mixing. The strongest winds buffeted the area for a 12-18 hour period, as the initial surge arrived during the pre-dawn hours. Most locations didn't reach peak intensity until late morning or early afternoon, and speeds quickly subsided by sunset or shortly thereafter. Attesting to the strength of the surface pressure gradient, 8 AM CST observations revealed an 18-millibar gradient across the state of Kansas, ranging from 1027mb in the far northwest corner of the state to around 1009mb in the far southeast.  ||The combination of these strong winds, along with very low relative humidity values dropping to near 20% or lower, also resulted in elevated to critical fire danger across much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated stations near the county recorded sustained winds in excess of 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of dry weather continued unabated across portions of Central and South Central Kansas during the month of September 2011, as most of the area saw below normal levels of precipitation and even saw a few more triple digit temperatures for the beginning of the month. Some rainfall did fall during the middle of the month which brought some relief, but overall rainfall was disappointing, with most areas seeing rainfall amounts two inches below normal. Areas south and west of Wichita continued to remain very dry, keeping this area in an exceptional drought (D4), while others areas across Central Kansas remain in an extreme drought (D3). Other areas across the Flint Hills continued with a severe drought (D2) throughout the month. This is a continuation of the widespread drought that has been on going across the Plains for the entire summer of 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought (D2), continued across Southeast Kansas during the month of September. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late July.  Local cattle farmers had sold most of their cattle due to poor pasture conditions with little or no hay to feed them.  Due to the high demand, hay prices almost doubled in the past 12 months. Local grain elevators reported received only half as much grain as it does in a normal year, due to the drought.

An upper level trough of low pressure moved across Northwest Kansas bringing snowfall amounts of six to almost eleven inches with it. The snowfall began during the early morning, then began to taper off during the afternoon and evening hours. The highest snowfall amounts were observed along a line from near Goodland where 6.0 inches were reported, northeast to Herndon where 12.0 inches were reported.  I-70 from Colby to the Colorado border was closed through the evening due to snow packed roads causing multiple accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured snowfall report of 6.5 inches reported in Goodland.  At 8 NE, 12 NE and 11 SW of Goodland, 6.0 inches of snowfall was measured.  A semi jackknifed into the median near the Sherman-Thomas County line on I-70.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of dry weather eased some across portions of Central and South Central Kansas during the month of November 2011. Some welcome rainfall, in the form of thunderstorms, did fall across most of the region during the beginning of the month. This brought some drought relief, with most areas seeing their first above normal rainfall in almost a year.  More welcome rainfall occurred at the end of the month as well.  Portions of South Central and Southern Kansas saw their first drought improvement in almost six months, as some areas southwest of Wichita went from an exceptional drought (D4) to an extreme drought (D3).  Other areas of southern Kansas improved from an extreme drought (D3) to a severe drought (D2).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought (D2) continued across Central Kansas during the first part of the month, but rainfall early in the month and late in the month actually improved the drought conditions by the middle of November. The drought remained at severe levels (D2) across the area through November 22nd.

Severe drought remained in place across portions southwestern Missouri as rain fell sparingly during the month. Rainfall was near half of normal for the month. Locations that received this precipitation saw it fall in just a few events with the majority of the month dry. This helped keep the region from falling into deeper drought, but did not allow for much improvement in area dry conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S Drought Monitor reported continued deterioration from Moderate (D1) to Extreme Drought (D2) throughout the month of October across the county. Rainfall below 50 percent of normal was recorded during the month which allow drought conditions to continue across the county. This is a continuation of the drought that began across the region in July.

On July 27, 28, and 29, multiple thunderstorms developed near a slowly moving cold front which spread across northeast Kansas. The very warm surface conditions led to an environment that was supportive of downbursts. A few of the thunderstorms quickly intensified, but with lack of any mid-level shear to support the updrafts; the storms quickly collapsed upon themselves, bringing strong downpours and damaging winds. The most intense of these downburst thunderstorms formed in Ottawa County and brought 70 to 80 mph winds to areas near Wells and Oak Hill.  Numerous outbuildings were destroyed and trees uprooted as a result of the damaging wind gusts. Areas of Riley and Pottawatomie Counties also recieved strong winds, and in Jefferson County, a boating dock broke loose from the Lake Perry Marina.

An extended period of dry weather eased some across portions of Central and South Central Kansas during the month of November 2011. Some welcome rainfall, in the form of thunderstorms, did fall across most of the region during the beginning of the month. This brought some drought relief, with most areas seeing their first above normal rainfall in almost a year.  More welcome rainfall occurred at the end of the month as well.  Portions of South Central and Southern Kansas saw their first drought improvement in almost six months, as some areas southwest of Wichita went from an exceptional drought (D4) to an extreme drought (D3).  Other areas of southern Kansas improved from an extreme drought (D3) to a severe drought (D2).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) continued across Central Kansas during the first part of the month, but rainfall early in the month and late in the month actually improved the drought conditions by the end of November. The drought remained at extreme levels (D3) across the area through November 22nd, but eased to an severe drought (D2) by the end of the month. This was first improvement in the drought conditions since the drought began in late April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure moved across Northwest Kansas bringing snowfall amounts of six to almost eleven inches with it. The snowfall began during the early morning, then began to taper off during the afternoon and evening hours. The highest snowfall amounts were observed along a line from near Goodland where 6.0 inches were reported, northeast to Herndon where 12.0 inches were reported.  I-70 from Colby to the Colorado border was closed through the evening due to snow packed roads causing multiple accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured snowfall amount of 7.5 inches was reported at Dresden.  At 2 miles north of Traer, snowfall measured 10.7 inches.  Elsewhere 9.0 inches was measured at 6 S of Lebanon, 7.0 inches was reported in Oberlin and 8.0 inches was measured at Cedar Bluffs; all with some snow still falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with heavy snowfall brought blizzard conditions to parts of Northwest Kansas.  The snowfall began during the late morning of the 19th, lasting into the morning of the 20th.  Frequent reports of visibilities of a quarter mile or less were reported with this storm.  The highest snowfall amounts occurred over Greeley and Wichita counties where almost 13.5 and 11 inches were reported respectively.  Snowfall amounts of seven to nine inches quickly declined to two or three inches from south to north over Wallace, Logan, and Gove counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions reported 14 S of Leoti with an estimated four inches of snow on the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Saturday of Thanksgiving weekend was one of the overall windiest days of fall 2011 across this portion of North Central Kansas, especially for areas near and west of Highway 281, where there were several hours of sustained north-northwest winds around 40 MPH accompanied by gusts around 50 MPH.||These winds occurred in the wake of a strong upper level low pressure system and associated powerful surface cold front, and were driven by the combination of a tight surface pressure gradient and deep mixing. The strongest winds buffeted the area for a 12-18 hour period, as the initial surge arrived during the pre-dawn hours. Most locations didn't reach peak intensity until late morning or early afternoon, and speeds quickly subsided by sunset or shortly thereafter. Attesting to the strength of the surface pressure gradient, 8 AM CST observations revealed an 18-millibar gradient across the state of Kansas, ranging from 1027mb in the far northwest corner of the state to around 1009mb in the far southeast.  ||The combination of these strong winds, along with very low relative humidity values dropping to near 20% or lower, also resulted in elevated to critical fire danger across much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated stations near the county recorded sustained winds in excess of 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of dry weather eased some across portions of Central and South Central Kansas during the month of November 2011. Some welcome rainfall, in the form of thunderstorms, did fall across most of the region during the beginning of the month. This brought some drought relief, with most areas seeing their first above normal rainfall in almost a year.  More welcome rainfall occurred at the end of the month as well.  Portions of South Central and Southern Kansas saw their first drought improvement in almost six months, as some areas southwest of Wichita went from an exceptional drought (D4) to an extreme drought (D3).  Other areas of southern Kansas improved from an extreme drought (D3) to a severe drought (D2).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) continued across Southeast Kansas during the first part of the month, but rainfall early in the month and late in the month actually improved the drought conditions by the end of November. The drought remained at extreme levels (D3) across the area through November 22nd, but eased to an severe drought (D2) by the end of the month. This was first improvement in the drought conditions since the drought began in late April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with heavy snowfall brought blizzard conditions to parts of Northwest Kansas.  The snowfall began during the late morning of the 19th, lasting into the morning of the 20th.  Frequent reports of visibilities of a quarter mile or less were reported with this storm.  The highest snowfall amounts occurred over Greeley and Wichita counties where almost 13.5 and 11 inches were reported respectively.  Snowfall amounts of seven to nine inches quickly declined to two or three inches from south to north over Wallace, Logan, and Gove counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions reported 5 WSW of Russell Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 27, 28, and 29, multiple thunderstorms developed near a slowly moving cold front which spread across northeast Kansas. The very warm surface conditions led to an environment that was supportive of downbursts. A few of the thunderstorms quickly intensified, but with lack of any mid-level shear to support the updrafts; the storms quickly collapsed upon themselves, bringing strong downpours and damaging winds. The most intense of these downburst thunderstorms formed in Ottawa County and brought 70 to 80 mph winds to areas near Wells and Oak Hill.  Numerous outbuildings were destroyed and trees uprooted as a result of the damaging wind gusts. Areas of Riley and Pottawatomie Counties also recieved strong winds, and in Jefferson County, a boating dock broke loose from the Lake Perry Marina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter estimates winds of 60 mph.

Severe drought remained in place across portions southwestern Missouri as rain fell sparingly during the month. Rainfall was near half of normal for the month. Locations that received this precipitation saw it fall in just a few events with the majority of the month dry. This helped keep the region from falling into deeper drought, but did not allow for much improvement in area dry conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S Drought Monitor continued to report Moderate (D1) to Severe Drought (D2) throughout the month of October across the county. Rainfall below 50 percent of normal was recorded during the month which allow drought conditions to continue across the county. This is a continuation of the drought that began across the region in July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Saturday of Thanksgiving weekend was one of the overall windiest days of fall 2011 across this portion of North Central Kansas, especially for areas near and west of Highway 281, where there were several hours of sustained north-northwest winds around 40 MPH accompanied by gusts around 50 MPH.||These winds occurred in the wake of a strong upper level low pressure system and associated powerful surface cold front, and were driven by the combination of a tight surface pressure gradient and deep mixing. The strongest winds buffeted the area for a 12-18 hour period, as the initial surge arrived during the pre-dawn hours. Most locations didn't reach peak intensity until late morning or early afternoon, and speeds quickly subsided by sunset or shortly thereafter. Attesting to the strength of the surface pressure gradient, 8 AM CST observations revealed an 18-millibar gradient across the state of Kansas, ranging from 1027mb in the far northwest corner of the state to around 1009mb in the far southeast.  ||The combination of these strong winds, along with very low relative humidity values dropping to near 20% or lower, also resulted in elevated to critical fire danger across much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated stations near the county recorded sustained winds in excess of 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Wednesday featured the first measurable snow of the 2011-2012 winter season for portions of North Central Kansas, with wet, slushy accumulations as high as 2-4 inches falling within a narrow corridor no more than 10 miles across in most areas. The center of this narrow snow band stretched from Phillips County across southern Smith and far northern Osborne Counties, and bisected Mitchell County from northwest to southeast. Although most places within this band averaged less than 2 inches, isolated areas received more, with photos from Downs revealing at least 3-4 inches. In contrast, locations just outside the primary snow band saw no more than flurries up to possibly a few tenths of an inch. ||Timing-wise, most of this snow fell during a five-hour window between 9 AM and 2 PM CST, with the primary mid-upper level forcing mechanism consisting of frontogenesis near 700 millibars, focused within the entrance region of a strong upper level jet streak centered from Iowa across the Great Lakes. With temperatures already the following day climbing into the low-mid 50s, the snow from this early-season event did not hang around long.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 2 to 4 inches were reported across parts of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of dry weather continued unabated across portions of Central and South Central Kansas during the month of September 2011, as most of the area saw below normal levels of precipitation and even saw a few more triple digit temperatures for the beginning of the month. Some rainfall did fall during the middle of the month which brought some relief, but overall rainfall was disappointing, with most areas seeing rainfall amounts two inches below normal. Areas south and west of Wichita continued to remain very dry, keeping this area in an exceptional drought (D4), while others areas across Central Kansas remain in an extreme drought (D3). Other areas across the Flint Hills continued with a severe drought (D2) throughout the month. This is a continuation of the widespread drought that has been on going across the Plains for the entire summer of 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The exceptional drought (D4) continued across South Central Kansas during the first part of the month and lasted the rest of the month of September. The drought reached exceptional levels (D4) across the area during the week of July 19th. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late April and continued through the Summer into August and September. Local cattle farmers had sold most of their cattle due to poor pasture conditions with little or no hay to feed them.  Due to the high demand, hay prices almost doubled in the past 12 months.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of dry weather eased some across portions of Central and South Central Kansas during the month of November 2011. Some welcome rainfall, in the form of thunderstorms, did fall across most of the region during the beginning of the month. This brought some drought relief, with most areas seeing their first above normal rainfall in almost a year.  More welcome rainfall occurred at the end of the month as well.  Portions of South Central and Southern Kansas saw their first drought improvement in almost six months, as some areas southwest of Wichita went from an exceptional drought (D4) to an extreme drought (D3).  Other areas of southern Kansas improved from an extreme drought (D3) to a severe drought (D2).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought (D2) continued across Central Kansas during the first part of the month, but rainfall early in the month and late in the month actually improved the drought conditions by the middle of November. The drought remained at severe levels (D2) across the area through November 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds behind a cold front moving southeast through eastern Colorado caused winds to gust over 70 MPH during the late morning and afternoon. The high winds blew dirt and sand, which reduced visibilities to a quarter mile at times and caused I-70 to be closed, knocked down power poles and trees, and took the roof off at least one home.  Some of the higher gusts that were reported were 78 MPH at 12 SE of Sharon Springs, 77 MPH at Goodland, 70 MPH at Hoxie, 68 MPH at Weskan, 67 MPH at Bird City, 66 MPH 4 NW of Marienthal, and 64 MPH 4 N of Atwood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at Grinnell.  Relayed by KSN-TV.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell thunderstorm moved from Imperial, Nebraska southeast to Benkelman, and traveled across northwest Kansas through Bird City, McDonald, to Colby and Oakley before weakening across Gove county Kansas.  Widespread wind and hail damage was reported with the storm through the late afternoon hours.  A second strong thunderstorm moved southeast during the evening, producing large hail over Hitchcock county Nebraska and Norton county Kansas.

On July 27, 28, and 29, multiple thunderstorms developed near a slowly moving cold front which spread across northeast Kansas. The very warm surface conditions led to an environment that was supportive of downbursts. A few of the thunderstorms quickly intensified, but with lack of any mid-level shear to support the updrafts; the storms quickly collapsed upon themselves, bringing strong downpours and damaging winds. The most intense of these downburst thunderstorms formed in Ottawa County and brought 70 to 80 mph winds to areas near Wells and Oak Hill.  Numerous outbuildings were destroyed and trees uprooted as a result of the damaging wind gusts. Areas of Riley and Pottawatomie Counties also recieved strong winds, and in Jefferson County, a boating dock broke loose from the Lake Perry Marina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines down and tree damage on the south side of Ogden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of dry weather continued unabated across portions of Central and South Central Kansas during the month of October 2011, as most of the area saw below normal levels of precipitation. Some rainfall did fall across South Central Kansas during the beginning of the month, which brought some relief, but overall rainfall was disappointing, with most areas seeing rainfall amounts one to three inches below normal. Areas south and west of Wichita continued to remain very dry, keeping this area in an exceptional drought (D4), while others areas across Central Kansas remain in an extreme drought (D3). Other areas across the Flint Hills dried out even further during the second half of the month going from a severe drought (D2) to an extreme drought (D3). This is a continuation of the widespread drought that has been on going across the Plains for the past six months.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3) continued across South Central Kansas during the first part of the month and lasted the rest of the month of October.  The drought reached extreme levels (D3) across the area during the week of August 30th. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late April and continued through the summer months into October. Due to the high demand, hay prices almost doubled in the past 12 months. Winter wheat planting increased, as farmers planted hundreds of thousands more acres, through failed corn and soybean crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slippery road surfaces, attributable to the arrival of a winter storm a few days earlier in concert with very light snow from the morning into the early afternoon on the 22nd, contributed to a rash of automobile accidents, slide-offs and roll-overs across central Kansas on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slick roads contributed to a rollover accident on I-70 near milepost 217 just after noon on the 22nd, resulting in the injuries (indirect) of a 23-year-old man and a 23-year-old woman. Information courtesy of the Ellsworth County Independent/Reporter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds of 50 to 60 mph swept southeast across central and south-central Kansas during the evening of the 31st, in wake of a strong cold front. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were likely sustained around 40 mph, with a few gusts near 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved northeast from New Mexico into Colorado Friday, December 2nd and into western Kansas Friday night.  The precipitation began as rain across western and northern parts of the DDC CWA, and began to change to snow and patchy freezing drizzle Friday night into Saturday, December 3rd.  The stormed tracked to north central Kansas by Saturday night.  Before it departed, up to 5 inches of snow fell in parts of central Kansas.  The 5-inch maximum of snow fell across northwestern Trego County, mainly west of Wakeeney.  A narrow band (10-20 miles wide) of 3 to 4 inches of snow fell from extreme northwest Ellis County southwestward through central Trego County and then west over northern parts of both Lane and Scott Counties.  Southeast of this heavier snow, there was a 30 to 50 mile wide swath of one to two inches of snow that fell from near Hays in Ellis County to near Ness City in Ness County to northwestern Finney County and then on southwestward to near Syracuse in Hamilton County.  Early in the event, mainly during Friday night, there was patchy freezing drizzle mainly in Ellis, Trego and Scott Counties, which caused a few slick spots on roadways. |||12/19/11 1200 CST to 12/20/11 2100 CST||Barber-Comanche-Clark-Edwards-Ellis-Finney-Ford-Grant-Gray-Hamilton-Haskell- Hodgeman-    Kearny-Kiowa-Lane-Meade-Morton-Ness-Pawnee-Pratt-Rush-Scott-Seward-Stafford-Stanton-Stevens-Trego||A very strong upper level winter storm moved from southern New Mexico into Western Kansas on Monday, December 19th.  Late Monday, a strong cold front crashed southward from Wyoming and Nebraska, colliding with the abundant moisture the upper level storm brought to western Kansas.  Strong winds behind the cold front blew from the north at 20 to 30 MPH, with gusts as high as 45 MPH, mainly Monday night and early Tuesday.  Full white-out blizzard conditions were reported by many county officials, especially north of a line from Johnson City to Dodge City to Kinsley, Monday night and into Tuesday, December 20th.  Numerous accidents were reported, as well as were snow drifts as deep as 2 to 5 feet during and after the blizzard.||Over much of the affected area, there was one or more periods of light freezing drizzle mixed in with the snow, which served to make many roadways slick.  The heavy snow fell in 10 to 25 mile wide band, and in the shape of an arch, from Elkhart in Morton County northward to Syracuse in Hamilton County, then northeast to near Scott City in Scott County, then eastward to east of Hays in Ellis County.  There were reports of more than 2 feet of snow along the Colorado/Kansas border in western Stanton county.  The highest official snowfall report of 15 inches was from 10 miles southeast of Scott City.  The second highest report was 14 inches from Victoria in Ellis County.  Over 10 inches fell in the band described above, with another noteworthy report of 12 inches from Elkhart in Morton County.  A large area received from 4 to 9 inches east of the heavier band mentioned above, and also west of a line from Englewood in southern Clark County, to Bucklin in southern Ford County, to Kinsley in Edwards County, to near Victoria in eastern Ellis County.  One to Three inches of snow fell east of the 4 to 8 inch area, but no snow accumulated in Barber County.

A narrow band (10-20 miles wide) of 3 to 4 inches of snow fell from extreme northwest Ellis County southwestward through central Trego County and then west over northern parts of both Lane and Scott Counties.  Southeast of this heavier snow, there was a 30 to 50 mile wide swath of one to two inches of snow that fell from near Hays in Ellis County to near Ness City in Ness County to northwestern Finney County and then on southwestward to near Syracuse in Hamilton County. Full white-out blizzard conditions were reported by many county officials, especially north of a line from Johnson City to Dodge City to Kinsley, Monday night and into Tuesday, December 20th. There were reports of more than 2 feet of snow along the Colorado/Kansas border in western Stanton county.  The highest official snowfall report of 15 inches was from 10 miles southeast of Scott City.  The second highest report was 14 inches from Victoria in Ellis County.  Over 10 inches fell in the band described above, with another noteworthy report of 12 inches from Elkhart in Morton County.  A large area received from 4 to 9 inches east of the heavier band mentioned above, and also west of a line from Englewood in southern Clark County, to Bucklin in southern Ford County, to Kinsley in Edwards County, to near Victoria in eastern Ellis County.  One to Three inches of snow fell east of the 4 to 8 inch area, but no snow accumulated in Barber County.
A line of storms produced sub-severe hail across portions of Central Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of dry weather continued unabated across portions of Central and South Central Kansas during the month of October 2011, as most of the area saw below normal levels of precipitation. Some rainfall did fall across South Central Kansas during the beginning of the month, which brought some relief, but overall rainfall was disappointing, with most areas seeing rainfall amounts one to three inches below normal. Areas south and west of Wichita continued to remain very dry, keeping this area in an exceptional drought (D4), while others areas across Central Kansas remain in an extreme drought (D3). Other areas across the Flint Hills dried out even further during the second half of the month going from a severe drought (D2) to an extreme drought (D3). This is a continuation of the widespread drought that has been on going across the Plains for the past six months.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought (D2) began to worsen across Southeast Kansas during the first part of October and lasted the rest of the month. The drought reached extreme levels (D3) across the area during the week of October 18th.

Winter wheat planting increased, as farmers planted hundreds of thousands more acres, through failed corn and soybean crops.

An extended period of dry weather continued unabated across portions of Central and South Central Kansas during the month of October 2011, as most of the area saw below normal levels of precipitation. Some rainfall did fall across South Central Kansas during the beginning of the month, which brought some relief, but overall rainfall was disappointing, with most areas seeing rainfall amounts one to three inches below normal. Areas south and west of Wichita continued to remain very dry, keeping this area in an exceptional drought (D4), while others areas across Central Kansas remain in an extreme drought (D3). Other areas across the Flint Hills dried out even further during the second half of the month going from a severe drought (D2) to an extreme drought (D3). This is a continuation of the widespread drought that has been on going across the Plains for the past six months.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional drought (D4) continued across South Central Kansas during the first part of the month and lasted the rest of the month of September. The drought reached exceptional levels (D4) across the area during the week of July 19th. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late April and continued through the summer into October.   Due to the high demand, hay prices almost doubled in the past 12 months. Winter wheat planting increased, as farmers planted hundreds of thousands more acres, through failed corn and soybean crops.

A weak area of low pressure tracked along the Kansas Oklahoma border during the afternoon and evening hours of November 7th, 2011.  As this low pressure area moved along, low level moisture increased along a warm frontal boundary across Southern Kansas to produce a squall line of thunderstorms across Southern Kansas.  This squall line produced some bowing segments along it, to produce some isolated severe wind gusts and isolated large hail reports up to golfball size.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Russell Airport ASOS measured a few wind gusts around 60 mph during the mid-afternoon hours. No reports of damage were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A linear convective complex stretching from northwestern Arkansas into Missouri pushed to the north northeast during the evening hours. The line of thunderstorms produced a few reports of downed trees due to thunderstorm wind gust as well as from a well defined outflow boundary that ran out ahead of the storm complex. Hail to the size of quarters was also reported in Pittsburg, Kansas from the system.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 MPH wind gust was measured at the Ulysses airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level low pressure area across the central Rockies moved east across Kansas and Oklahoma on Tuesday, February 8th. At the same time, frigid Arctic air settled south across the Central Plains.  A rather narrow, but strong corridor of lift developed out ahead of the approaching system early on Tuesday February 8th, resulting in a band of heavy snowfall, oriented west-northwest to east-southeast, across Central Kansas. This resulted in snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour during the late morning and early afternoon on Tuesday. The snow diminished to flurries on Tuesday night, but not before it dumped over a foot of snow across much of Central Kansas and a portion of the Flint Hills. ||The strong area of mid-level lift associated with the upper level storm system gradually shifted south across South Central and Southeast Kansas on Tuesday night, February 8th. Since the lift was more transient and did not reside over any one location too long, snowfall totals were not as impressive as across Central Kansas, though still managing to range from 5 to 10 inches across South Central Kansas and Southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 11 inches across the county, with the heaviest snow amounts to the northeast of Salina.  The snow drifts were over a foot deep in some locations, which led to numerous cars becoming stuck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late morning on the 17th, strong wind gusts out of the west to northwest associated with a passing cold front downed a large bus barn under construction in Russell. No injuries occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the late morning on the 17th, strong wind gusts out of the west to northwest around 40 mph associated with a passing cold front downed a large bus barn under construction in Russell. The wind gust was measured by ASOS equipment at the Russell Airport. No injuries occurred. Property damage amounts are rough estimates. The Russell County News contributed to this report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on Tuesday, February 8th, a strong, Arctic cold front plunged south through southwest Kansas as a strong upper system swept through New Mexico and eastward across Oklahoma.  These two weather features resulted in cold air being overrun by abundant low level moisture.  Snow fell, heavy at times, through Wednesday morning before moving east of the area.  From 10 to 13 inches of snow fell northeast of a line from northern Pratt County near Byers to southeastern Hodgeman County just southeast of Jetmore to near Beeler in Ness County.  The highest amount reported from this event was 13 inches just east of Trego Center in Trego County.  From 6 to 10-inches fell over a wide area basically east of a line from Scott City in Scott County to near Charleston in northwestern Gray County to around Meade State Park in Meade County.  West of this line, 4-inches of less of snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 11.0 inches fell at a location 4 miles northwest of Cedar Bluff Reservoir.

An intense, slow-moving, winter-like storm moved across the Central High Plains on April 14-15.  The storm produced a round of high winds with numerous gusts over 60 mph, in addition to wet, heavy snow.  Blizzard conditions occurred in some areas with Interstate 70 and state highways closed.  Many residents were without power for periods of time ranging from 12 to 48 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet observations showing wind gusts to 60 mph were common across neighboring counties, and gusts to 60 mph are estimated to have occurred across Decatur county as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense, slow-moving, winter-like storm moved across the Central High Plains on April 14-15.  The storm produced a round of high winds with numerous gusts over 60 mph, in addition to wet, heavy snow.  Blizzard conditions occurred in some areas with Interstate 70 and state highways closed.  Many residents were without power for periods of time ranging from 12 to 48 hours.

For much of North Central Kansas, the heaviest snow event of the 2010-2011 winter season began during the evening hours on Monday the 7th, and lasted through much of the daytime and evening hours on Tuesday the 8th. Snowfall really got going during the pre-dawn hours on the 8th as an upper level trough and its associated strong mid-level frontogenesis set up a generally east-west snow band, which gradually sunk south across the area during the day. Already by sunrise, reports of 2-5 inches of snow were common, and additional snow during the day pushed storm totals into the 4-6 inch range across most of Phillips, Smith and Jewell Counties, and higher amounts of 6-9 inches across parts of Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell Counties. Specifically, a sampling of storm total amounts ranged from 5 inches at Ionia, 7.4 inches near Plainville, and up to 9 inches at Beloit. The area narrowly missed higher amounts of 10-15 inches that targeted parts of Central Kansas. Although winds were not overly strong during this event, northeasterly gusts of at least 20-25 MPH were not uncommon, resulting in plenty of drifting in areas of heavier snowfall due to its dry, powdery nature. In addition, in terms of daytime high temperatures, the 8th ended up being the coldest day of the winter for most of North Central Kansas, with most places only topping out between 3-5 degrees above zero. When factoring in winds, wind chill temperatures of -10 to -20 were common throughout this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm snowfall totals of 6 to 9 inches were reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms created a large swath of golf ball to baseball size hail across portions of South Central Kansas on Friday, April 8th 2011. Storms first developed over northwest and north-central Oklahoma along a dryline during the early evening hours of April 8th.  With abundant instability, the storms rapidly became severe as they tracked slowly east-northeast. Many of the large hail producing storms that affected South Central Kansas were storms that split from the large eastward moving supercells over northern Oklahoma.
