A extremely cold arctic airmass settled over the region in the aftermath of the snowstorm of February 8th and 9th.  As this area of arctic high pressure settled over the eastern half of Kansas and eastern Oklahoma, the combination of the cold air and fresh snow cover of 5 to 12 inches, led to optimal radiational cooling conditions on the morning of February 10th, with record minimum temperatures broken across the region.  Some locations in South Central and Southeast Kansas saw some of their coldest temperatures in 29 years.  Iola, Kansas recorded the coldest temperature in that cities history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The minimum temperature fell to minus 15 degrees in Chanute, Kansas, setting a record low temperature for this date.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a brief warm spell in late January, winter quickly returned to North Central Kansas on the last day of the month as a large upper level low pressure system carved into the central United States, bringing some of the coldest air of the season with it. On Monday January 31st, the day started out with widespread light freezing drizzle across the area, with slick roads contributing to a single-fatality car accident in Smith County. As the day wore on into the evening, the freezing drizzle gave way to light snow, with accumulations of 1-3 inches by midnight in some locations. Areas of mainly light snow continued into Tuesday February 1st, with storm total accumulations of 1-3 inches common across much of North Central Kansas. However, the primary story on the 1st became widespread visibility restrictions and at least minor drifting from blowing snow, thanks to strong north winds sustained at speeds to around 30 MPH and gusts to over 40 MPH. Near-blizzard conditions were noted for several hours during the day, especially along and east of Highway 281 where visibilities to 1/2 mile or less were common. In addition to snowfall impacts, Feb. 1st ended up being one of the coldest days of the winter for North Central Kansas, both in terms of actual air temperature and wind chill readings. Actual high temperatures on the 1st were mainly between 7-10 degrees, and when factoring in the winds, automated stations including Smith Center and Phillipsburg recorded wind chills down to at least -26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall accumulations of 2 to 4 inches combined with strong winds of 25 to 30 mph and periodic gusts approaching 45 mph brought reduced visibilities, occasional white out conditions and hazardous travel to the county.  Bitterly cold temperatures combined with the strong winds also brought wind chill vales which reached as low as 26 degrees below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms created a large swath of golf ball to baseball size hail across portions of South Central Kansas on Friday, April 8th 2011. Storms first developed over northwest and north-central Oklahoma along a dryline during the early evening hours of April 8th.  With abundant instability, the storms rapidly became severe as they tracked slowly east-northeast. Many of the large hail producing storms that affected South Central Kansas were storms that split from the large eastward moving supercells over northern Oklahoma.

A massive winter storm of historic proportions affected the region from the early morning hours January 31st through the evening hours February 1st. ||As this storm moved to the east of the area sustained north to northwest winds of 25 mph, with gusts up to 45 mph, produced near-blizzard conditions at times, along with extensive drifts, some of which exceeded 4 feet tall. Additionally, temperatures in the single digits and teens in concert with the strong winds resulted in wind chill readings as cold as  minus 30 degrees, with the coldest readings over Central Kansas.  Low temperature reading on the morning of February 3rd, ranged from minus 7 degrees in Newton, Kansas to minus 17 degrees in Smolan, Kansas.  Unfortunately one person did die from cold exposure in Coffeyville, Kansas, after she fell while removing snow from her backyard, and was unable to get back up.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme cold temperatures moved across the county during the overnight hours of February 1st and 2nd and early on February 3rd.  Wind chill readings dropped as low as minus 20 degees during the evening of February 2nd, with the low temperatures falling to minus 5 degrees in Wichita, Kansas on the morning of February 3rd.  The extreme cold caused most of the school districts in the surrounding communities to close on February 1st and 2nd.  The campus at Wichita State University also cancelled classes on February 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on Tuesday, February 8th, a strong, Arctic cold front plunged south through southwest Kansas as a strong upper system swept through New Mexico and eastward across Oklahoma.  These two weather features resulted in cold air being overrun by abundant low level moisture.  Snow fell, heavy at times, through Wednesday morning before moving east of the area.  From 10 to 13 inches of snow fell northeast of a line from northern Pratt County near Byers to southeastern Hodgeman County just southeast of Jetmore to near Beeler in Ness County.  The highest amount reported from this event was 13 inches just east of Trego Center in Trego County.  From 6 to 10-inches fell over a wide area basically east of a line from Scott City in Scott County to near Charleston in northwestern Gray County to around Meade State Park in Meade County.  West of this line, 4-inches of less of snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 7.0 fell at a location 5 miles east of Toulon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For much of North Central Kansas, the heaviest snow event of the 2010-2011 winter season began during the evening hours on Monday the 7th, and lasted through much of the daytime and evening hours on Tuesday the 8th. Snowfall really got going during the pre-dawn hours on the 8th as an upper level trough and its associated strong mid-level frontogenesis set up a generally east-west snow band, which gradually sunk south across the area during the day. Already by sunrise, reports of 2-5 inches of snow were common, and additional snow during the day pushed storm totals into the 4-6 inch range across most of Phillips, Smith and Jewell Counties, and higher amounts of 6-9 inches across parts of Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell Counties. Specifically, a sampling of storm total amounts ranged from 5 inches at Ionia, 7.4 inches near Plainville, and up to 9 inches at Beloit. The area narrowly missed higher amounts of 10-15 inches that targeted parts of Central Kansas. Although winds were not overly strong during this event, northeasterly gusts of at least 20-25 MPH were not uncommon, resulting in plenty of drifting in areas of heavier snowfall due to its dry, powdery nature. In addition, in terms of daytime high temperatures, the 8th ended up being the coldest day of the winter for most of North Central Kansas, with most places only topping out between 3-5 degrees above zero. When factoring in winds, wind chill temperatures of -10 to -20 were common throughout this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm snowfall totals of 4 to 6 inches were reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a brief warm spell in late January, winter quickly returned to North Central Kansas on the last day of the month as a large upper level low pressure system carved into the central United States, bringing some of the coldest air of the season with it. On Monday January 31st, the day started out with widespread light freezing drizzle across the area, with slick roads contributing to a single-fatality car accident in Smith County. As the day wore on into the evening, the freezing drizzle gave way to light snow, with accumulations of 1-3 inches by midnight in some locations. Areas of mainly light snow continued into Tuesday February 1st, with storm total accumulations of 1-3 inches common across much of North Central Kansas. However, the primary story on the 1st became widespread visibility restrictions and at least minor drifting from blowing snow, thanks to strong north winds sustained at speeds to around 30 MPH and gusts to over 40 MPH. Near-blizzard conditions were noted for several hours during the day, especially along and east of Highway 281 where visibilities to 1/2 mile or less were common. In addition to snowfall impacts, Feb. 1st ended up being one of the coldest days of the winter for North Central Kansas, both in terms of actual air temperature and wind chill readings. Actual high temperatures on the 1st were mainly between 7-10 degrees, and when factoring in the winds, automated stations including Smith Center and Phillipsburg recorded wind chills down to at least -26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall accumulations of 1 to 3 inches, combined with strong winds of 25 to 30 mph and gusts approaching 45 mph at times, resulted in reduced visibilities and hazardous travel across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A extremely cold arctic airmass settled over the region in the aftermath of the snowstorm of February 8th and 9th.  As this area of arctic high pressure settled over the eastern half of Kansas and eastern Oklahoma, the combination of the cold air and fresh snow cover of 5 to 12 inches, led to optimal radiational cooling conditions on the morning of February 10th, with record minimum temperatures broken across the region.  Some locations in South Central and Southeast Kansas saw some of their coldest temperatures in 29 years.  Iola, Kansas recorded the coldest temperature in that cities history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The minimum temperature fell to minus 16 degrees in Hutchinson, Kansas. A COOP observer in Hutchinson, Kansas reported a low temperature of minus 17 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although it only affected a small sliver of North Central Kansas, this Thursday featured one of the most intense, narrow bands of snow of the 2010-2011 winter season within the area. The overall winter event centered squarely on the daytime hours, kicking into gear in the morning with an initial round of scattered showers producing brief periods of snow pellets and sleet. A more widespread snow became established by late morning into the afternoon, before pulling east out of the area by early evening. Although this storm featured a typical mid level trough traversing the area, there was enough instability aloft to trigger the development of narrow mesoscale band of snow, which curled south out of South Central Nebraska, primarily affecting Phillips County. At its peak intensity during the afternoon, this snow band was only 20 miles wide at most, with hourly snowfall rates easily 1-2 inches per hour for several hours. Storm total amounts under this band across mainly the northwest half of Phillips County measured at least 6-8 inches, including 8 inches at Long Island. Not suprisingly, snowfall totals tailed off considerably outside the intense snow band across the remainder of North Central Kansas, with the majority of the area reporting only 2-4 inches. Winds during this snow event were from the north-northeast, with sustained speeds averaging 15-20 MPH and gusts into the 25-30 MPH range, resulting in at least some drifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm snowfall totals of 8 to 10 inches were reported around the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm brought heavy wintry precipitation to the Missouri Ozarks and southeast Kansas on February 1, 2011.  This impressive winter storm brought heavy snow and blizzard conditions from Oklahoma through eastern Kansas and the northwest half of Missouri and into the western Great Lakes region.||Snowfall amounts ranged from around 20 to 24 inches in parts of west central into central Missouri to trace amounts over south central Missouri.  In addition to the heavy snowfall, winds of 15 to 30 mph with some gusts near 40 mph occurred during the day and nighttime hours of February 1st creating significant blowing and drifting of snow along with bitterly cold wind chills.  This created blizzard conditions with near zero visibility at times and snow drifts up to several feet.||Significant accumulations of sleet and freezing rain occurred across portions of southern Missouri. Sleet accumulations of one half to 2 inches fell along and just south of the Interstate 44 corridor.  Freezing rain accumulations ranged from one tenth to three quarters of an inch along and south of Interstate 44 with accumulations up to three quarters of an inch in south central Missouri into the eastern Missouri Ozarks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm produced heavy snow and blizzard conditions at times across extreme southeast Kansas.  Heavy snow accumulations of 14 to 20 inches were observed. Northwest winds of 20 to 40 mph resulted in drifts up to several feet and visibilities less than 100 feet. Travel was extremely treacherous with some roads impassable.

Wind chill readings dropped as low as minus 30 degees during the evening of February 2nd, with the low temperatures falling to minus 11 degrees in Ellsworth, Kansas, and minus 7 degrees at Kanopolis Lake, Kansas on the morning of February 3rd.

Additionally, temperatures in the single digits and teens in concert with the strong winds resulted in wind chill readings as cold as  minus 30 degrees, with the coldest readings over Central Kansas.  Low temperature reading on the morning of February 3rd, ranged from minus 7 degrees in Newton, Kansas to minus 17 degrees in Smolan, Kansas. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme cold temperatures moved across the county during the overnight hours of February 2nd and early on February 3rd.  Wind chill readings dropped as low as minus 20 degees during the evening of February 2nd, with the low temperatures falling to minus 8 degrees in Coffeyville, Kansas and minus 7 in Independence, Kansas on the morning of February 3rd.||Unfortunately, a 79 year old Coffeyville, Kansas woman died of exposure to the cold, after falling in her backyard while trying to clear snow on the morning of February 2nd.   She apparently was unable to get back up after falling, some 15 feet from her back step and died from exposure to the extreme cold temperatures.  Actual time of death is unknown, but law enforcement authorities say that she had been lying in the location for an extended period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although it only affected a small sliver of North Central Kansas, this Thursday featured one of the most intense, narrow bands of snow of the 2010-2011 winter season within the area. The overall winter event centered squarely on the daytime hours, kicking into gear in the morning with an initial round of scattered showers producing brief periods of snow pellets and sleet. A more widespread snow became established by late morning into the afternoon, before pulling east out of the area by early evening. Although this storm featured a typical mid level trough traversing the area, there was enough instability aloft to trigger the development of narrow mesoscale band of snow, which curled south out of South Central Nebraska, primarily affecting Phillips County. At its peak intensity during the afternoon, this snow band was only 20 miles wide at most, with hourly snowfall rates easily 1-2 inches per hour for several hours. Storm total amounts under this band across mainly the northwest half of Phillips County measured at least 6-8 inches, including 8 inches at Long Island. Not suprisingly, snowfall totals tailed off considerably outside the intense snow band across the remainder of North Central Kansas, with the majority of the area reporting only 2-4 inches. Winds during this snow event were from the north-northeast, with sustained speeds averaging 15-20 MPH and gusts into the 25-30 MPH range, resulting in at least some drifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm snowfall totals of 3 to 5 inches were reported around the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system moved across the southern plains region into the Ozarks region. A cold front moved down across the central plains into the Ozarks.  A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed along the front with a couple supercells ahead of the main line.  These severe storms caused damaging winds and large hail up to half dollars in size.

Late in the day on Friday April 8th, strong thunderstorms formed in southeast Kansas and pushed northward into northeast Kansas.  As these storms pushed northward they left a swath of hail across Morris county, with the largest stones measuring 3 in diameter. Later in the evening, around 10 pm a very strong thunderstorm moved northward out of Chase county into Morris county. Radar signatures indicated that a tornado may have spawned from the storm between Burdick and Hymer.  Despite the intense signature, no tornado has been confirmed due to the fact that the possible tornado occurred over open country and no one witnessed it. Other strong storms in Wabaunsee and Anderson counties also produced swaths of 1 inch to 1.5 inch hail during the evening of April 8.

A potent upper level low pressure area across the central Rockies moved east across Kansas and Oklahoma on Tuesday, February 8th. At the same time, frigid Arctic air settled south across the Central Plains.  A rather narrow, but strong corridor of lift developed out ahead of the approaching system early on Tuesday February 8th, resulting in a band of heavy snowfall, oriented west-northwest to east-southeast, across Central Kansas. This resulted in snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour during the late morning and early afternoon on Tuesday. The snow diminished to flurries on Tuesday night, but not before it dumped over a foot of snow across much of Central Kansas and a portion of the Flint Hills. ||The strong area of mid-level lift associated with the upper level storm system gradually shifted south across South Central and Southeast Kansas on Tuesday night, February 8th. Since the lift was more transient and did not reside over any one location too long, snowfall totals were not as impressive as across Central Kansas, though still managing to range from 5 to 10 inches across South Central Kansas and Southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of snow fell across the county, from the second powerful storm to hit the county in less than a week.

Severe storms created a large swath of golf ball to baseball size hail across portions of South Central Kansas on Friday, April 8th 2011. Storms first developed over northwest and north-central Oklahoma along a dryline during the early evening hours of April 8th.  With abundant instability, the storms rapidly became severe as they tracked slowly east-northeast. Many of the large hail producing storms that affected South Central Kansas were storms that split from the large eastward moving supercells over northern Oklahoma.

Multiple rounds of thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall across southeast Kansas over the course of a week. Some areas saw storm total rainfall amounts up to a foot or more.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Highway K-7 was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a brief warm spell in late January, winter quickly returned to North Central Kansas on the last day of the month as a large upper level low pressure system carved into the central United States, bringing some of the coldest air of the season with it. On Monday January 31st, the day started out with widespread light freezing drizzle across the area, with slick roads contributing to a single-fatality car accident in Smith County. As the day wore on into the evening, the freezing drizzle gave way to light snow, with accumulations of 1-3 inches by midnight in some locations. Areas of mainly light snow continued into Tuesday February 1st, with storm total accumulations of 1-3 inches common across much of North Central Kansas. However, the primary story on the 1st became widespread visibility restrictions and at least minor drifting from blowing snow, thanks to strong north winds sustained at speeds to around 30 MPH and gusts to over 40 MPH. Near-blizzard conditions were noted for several hours during the day, especially along and east of Highway 281 where visibilities to 1/2 mile or less were common. In addition to snowfall impacts, Feb. 1st ended up being one of the coldest days of the winter for North Central Kansas, both in terms of actual air temperature and wind chill readings. Actual high temperatures on the 1st were mainly between 7-10 degrees, and when factoring in the winds, automated stations including Smith Center and Phillipsburg recorded wind chills down to at least -26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall accumulations of 2 to 4 inches combined with strong winds of 25 to 30 mph and periodic gusts approaching 45 mph brought reduced visibilities, occasional white out conditions and hazardous travel to the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A extremely cold arctic airmass settled over the region in the aftermath of the snowstorm of February 8th and 9th.  As this area of arctic high pressure settled over the eastern half of Kansas and eastern Oklahoma, the combination of the cold air and fresh snow cover of 5 to 12 inches, led to optimal radiational cooling conditions on the morning of February 10th, with record minimum temperatures broken across the region.  Some locations in South Central and Southeast Kansas saw some of their coldest temperatures in 29 years.  Iola, Kansas recorded the coldest temperature in that cities history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer at the Iola Water plant recorded a record low temperature of minus 24 degrees.  This is the coldest temperature ever recorded in the city of Iola, Kansas, dating back to 1900.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For much of North Central Kansas, the heaviest snow event of the 2010-2011 winter season began during the evening hours on Monday the 7th, and lasted through much of the daytime and evening hours on Tuesday the 8th. Snowfall really got going during the pre-dawn hours on the 8th as an upper level trough and its associated strong mid-level frontogenesis set up a generally east-west snow band, which gradually sunk south across the area during the day. Already by sunrise, reports of 2-5 inches of snow were common, and additional snow during the day pushed storm totals into the 4-6 inch range across most of Phillips, Smith and Jewell Counties, and higher amounts of 6-9 inches across parts of Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell Counties. Specifically, a sampling of storm total amounts ranged from 5 inches at Ionia, 7.4 inches near Plainville, and up to 9 inches at Beloit. The area narrowly missed higher amounts of 10-15 inches that targeted parts of Central Kansas. Although winds were not overly strong during this event, northeasterly gusts of at least 20-25 MPH were not uncommon, resulting in plenty of drifting in areas of heavier snowfall due to its dry, powdery nature. In addition, in terms of daytime high temperatures, the 8th ended up being the coldest day of the winter for most of North Central Kansas, with most places only topping out between 3-5 degrees above zero. When factoring in winds, wind chill temperatures of -10 to -20 were common throughout this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm snowfall totals of 4 to 6 inches were reported across the county.

A periodic mix of freezing drizzle and light snow affected much of central, south-central and southeast Kansas from the predawn hours on the 19th to the early morning hours on the 20th. This resulted in very slick roads across the region, and contributed to numerous vehicle accidents, slide-offs and rollovers, several of which included injuries (indirect).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of freezing drizzle and light snow from the mid-afternoon hours on the 19th to the predawn hours on the 20th resulted in slick roads across the county, contributing to some vehicle accidents, slide-offs and rollovers. One injury (indirect) was reported one mile north of Zenda. The Kingman Leader-Courier contributed to this report.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported the gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in the day on Friday April 8th, strong thunderstorms formed in southeast Kansas and pushed northward into northeast Kansas.  As these storms pushed northward they left a swath of hail across Morris county, with the largest stones measuring 3 in diameter. Later in the evening, around 10 pm a very strong thunderstorm moved northward out of Chase county into Morris county. Radar signatures indicated that a tornado may have spawned from the storm between Burdick and Hymer.  Despite the intense signature, no tornado has been confirmed due to the fact that the possible tornado occurred over open country and no one witnessed it. Other strong storms in Wabaunsee and Anderson counties also produced swaths of 1 inch to 1.5 inch hail during the evening of April 8.

A periodic mix of freezing drizzle and light snow affected much of central, south-central and southeast Kansas from the predawn hours on the 19th to the early morning hours on the 20th. This resulted in very slick roads across the region, and contributed to numerous vehicle accidents, slide-offs and rollovers, several of which included injuries (indirect).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of freezing drizzle and light snow from the predawn to the late evening hours on the 19th resulted in slick roads across the county, contributing to some vehicle accidents, slide-offs and rollovers. Consequently, four injuries (indirect) were reported. The Great Bend Tribune contributed to this report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A massive winter storm of historic proportions affected the region from the early morning hours January 31st through the evening hours February 1st. ||As this storm moved to the east of the area sustained north to northwest winds of 25 mph, with gusts up to 45 mph, produced near-blizzard conditions at times, along with extensive drifts, some of which exceeded 4 feet tall. Additionally, temperatures in the single digits and teens in concert with the strong winds resulted in wind chill readings as cold as  minus 30 degrees, with the coldest readings over Central Kansas.  Low temperature reading on the morning of February 3rd, ranged from minus 7 degrees in Newton, Kansas to minus 17 degrees in Smolan, Kansas.  Unfortunately one person did die from cold exposure in Coffeyville, Kansas, after she fell while removing snow from her backyard, and was unable to get back up.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme cold temperatures moved across the county during the overnight hours of February 1st and 2nd and early on February 3rd.  Wind chill readings dropped as low as minus 30 degees during the evening of February 2nd, with the low temperatures falling to minus 7 degrees in Newton, Kansas on the morning of February 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A extremely cold arctic airmass settled over the region in the aftermath of the snowstorm of February 8th and 9th.  As this area of arctic high pressure settled over the eastern half of Kansas and eastern Oklahoma, the combination of the cold air and fresh snow cover of 5 to 12 inches, led to optimal radiational cooling conditions on the morning of February 10th, with record minimum temperatures broken across the region.  Some locations in South Central and Southeast Kansas saw some of their coldest temperatures in 29 years.  Iola, Kansas recorded the coldest temperature in that cities history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The minimum temperature fell to minus 22 degrees in Parsons, Kansas. A COOP observer in Mound Valley, Kansas reported a low temperature of minus 26 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a brief warm spell in late January, winter quickly returned to North Central Kansas on the last day of the month as a large upper level low pressure system carved into the central United States, bringing some of the coldest air of the season with it. On Monday January 31st, the day started out with widespread light freezing drizzle across the area, with slick roads contributing to a single-fatality car accident in Smith County. As the day wore on into the evening, the freezing drizzle gave way to light snow, with accumulations of 1-3 inches by midnight in some locations. Areas of mainly light snow continued into Tuesday February 1st, with storm total accumulations of 1-3 inches common across much of North Central Kansas. However, the primary story on the 1st became widespread visibility restrictions and at least minor drifting from blowing snow, thanks to strong north winds sustained at speeds to around 30 MPH and gusts to over 40 MPH. Near-blizzard conditions were noted for several hours during the day, especially along and east of Highway 281 where visibilities to 1/2 mile or less were common. In addition to snowfall impacts, Feb. 1st ended up being one of the coldest days of the winter for North Central Kansas, both in terms of actual air temperature and wind chill readings. Actual high temperatures on the 1st were mainly between 7-10 degrees, and when factoring in the winds, automated stations including Smith Center and Phillipsburg recorded wind chills down to at least -26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall accumulations of 1 to 3 inches, combined with strong winds of 25 to 30 mph and gusts approaching 45 mph at times, resulted in reduced visibilities and hazardous travel across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For much of North Central Kansas, the heaviest snow event of the 2010-2011 winter season began during the evening hours on Monday the 7th, and lasted through much of the daytime and evening hours on Tuesday the 8th. Snowfall really got going during the pre-dawn hours on the 8th as an upper level trough and its associated strong mid-level frontogenesis set up a generally east-west snow band, which gradually sunk south across the area during the day. Already by sunrise, reports of 2-5 inches of snow were common, and additional snow during the day pushed storm totals into the 4-6 inch range across most of Phillips, Smith and Jewell Counties, and higher amounts of 6-9 inches across parts of Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell Counties. Specifically, a sampling of storm total amounts ranged from 5 inches at Ionia, 7.4 inches near Plainville, and up to 9 inches at Beloit. The area narrowly missed higher amounts of 10-15 inches that targeted parts of Central Kansas. Although winds were not overly strong during this event, northeasterly gusts of at least 20-25 MPH were not uncommon, resulting in plenty of drifting in areas of heavier snowfall due to its dry, powdery nature. In addition, in terms of daytime high temperatures, the 8th ended up being the coldest day of the winter for most of North Central Kansas, with most places only topping out between 3-5 degrees above zero. When factoring in winds, wind chill temperatures of -10 to -20 were common throughout this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm snowfall totals of 6 to 9 inches were reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough across the central plains region and a strong upper level disturbance along with a frontal boundary in the area produced numerous severe storms with a few supercells across southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Funnel clouds were reported from the media and Storm Studio Live video feed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: April 3, 2011, was an abnormally warm day, with high temperatues in the 80s and 90s. Record high temperatures were broken in several locations. A slow moving cold front, combined with a very strong spring storm system, produced widespread damaging thunderstorms, in the late afternoon and evening hours. ||Supercellular thunderstorms, developed along and ahead of the cold front, as it sank southward across the area. These thunderstorms produced extremely large hail, greater than golf balls in many areas, as well as damaging winds of 60 to 80 mph. ||The hardest hit areas, included areas from Leavenworth County south to Linn County.

Multiple rounds of thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall across southeast Kansas over the course of a week.  A persistent trough over the central plains brought multiple upper level storm systems over the region which produced intense thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall.  Some areas saw storm total rainfall amounts up to a foot or more.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous low water crossings and rural routes were reported closed or impassable across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system passed north of the Tri-State region during the day, producing wind gusts of 50 to 70 mph.  Damage to tree limbs, utility poles and irrigation systems was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility was briefly reduced to one mile in blowing dirt.  Mesonet station 4 miles west of Goodland reported a peak wind of 66 mph at 1756 MDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system passed north of the Tri-State region during the day, producing wind gusts of 50 to 70 mph.  Damage to tree limbs, utility poles and irrigation systems was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS site in Atwood reported a peak wind gust of 60 mph at 1857 CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure over the western high plains resulted in very strong southerly winds across central and south-central Kansas from the late morning through the afternoon on the 29th. Winds were sustained at around 40 mph at times, with periodic gusts to around 60 mph. Some relatively light damage occurred across the area, ranging from downed trees and tree limbs, to a downed power pole. Unfortunately, one boating fatality occurred in Marion County. Furthermore, the strong winds helped fan a 300 acre wildfire over Reno County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured at the McPherson Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms continued to regenerate over the same area from mid-afternoon through early evening.  Numerous reports of half dollar to golf ball sized hail were received across Norton and Graham Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer initially reported golf ball sized hail at 1523 CST, and later called back stating that half dollar sized hail was still falling at 1528 CST.  Uncertain when hail actually ended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Thursday afternoon featured the only severe thunderstorms of the month across portions of North Central Kansas, with reports of hail from penny to half dollar size across several counties. These thunderstorms served as the opening volley to a very active few days of weather, as a classic, powerful, nearly vertically stacked low pressure system organized across western Kansas during the day. By early afternoon, a 998 millibar surface low was centered across south central Kansas, with a warm front extending from west to east across North Central Kansas. In the vicinity of this strengthening warm front, the mesoscale environment featured 500-1000 J/kg MLCAPE, and around 40-50 knots of deep layer vertical wind shear. In response to modest low level heating and destabilization within the northernmost reaches of the mid-level dry slot along with intense forcing from the mid-level low and considerable upper-level jet stream divergence, numerous strong to severe thunderstorms erupted by mid-afternoon, affecting much of the area for around 3 hours before sliding off to the north and east. Hail was the primary severe weather threat with these storms, with the largest reported hail of half dollar size occurring in Jewell and Smith Counties. Hail reportedly covered the ground in a few locations, including near Esbon and Athol. On a positive note, these storms brought parts of North Central Kansas some much-needed rainfall between 1-2 inches, with one of the highest reported amounts of 1.89 inches at Lebanon in Smith County.

A strong upper level disturbance and associated dryline approaching from the west interacted with an unstable air mass in place across the eastern half of Kansas, producing numerous strong to severe thunderstorms from the late afternoon on the 19th into the morning on the 20th over primarily central and north-central Kansas. A handful of relatively weak tornadoes touched down over Russell county, along with up to golfball size hail and 60 mph winds across the area. Additionally, very heavy rainfall produced flash flooding over portions of Russell and Lincoln counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado was on the ground in open country, causing minor damage to a shed and some trees.

Another downed tree damaged a car and a house in Midtown Wichita. Additionally, a large tree was downed near the intersection of Maple and Maize in West Wichita. Property damage amounts are rough estimates. The Wichita Eagle contributed to this report.

A frontal boundary approaching from the west interacted with an unstable air mass, sparking a few intense thunderstorms over far southeast Kansas during the afternoon and early evening of the 22nd. Hail up to the size of baseballs and softballs was reported south of Parsons, along with a few funnel clouds in the Parsons area.

Any property damage that may have occurred from the flooding is unknown.

During the day on Sunday, temperatures warmed to record highs across northeast Kansas before a powerful storm system moved through Sunday afternoon and evening.  Several locations reported hail up to 2 inches in diameter and winds of 70-80 mph with a few reports of structural damage due to straight line winds. Thunderstorms initiated late in the day behind a sharp cold front and quickly went severe. A well formed gustnado developed along the frontal boundary and rear flank boundary intersection near Williamstown on Highway 24. NWS survey crew identified areas of enhanced wind damage from a well formed gustnado in Williamstown. Two center pivot irrigations were flipped over, a large outdoor shed was destroyed and several tree limbs were snapped.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind damage reported in several locations across Anderson County. Roof removed from barn, windows broken out of home, shingles off of homes, and a missing carport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down at the intersection of 250th Street and Cavalry Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance and associated dryline approaching from the west interacted with an unstable air mass in place across the eastern half of Kansas, producing numerous strong to severe thunderstorms from the late afternoon on the 19th into the morning on the 20th over primarily central and north-central Kansas. A handful of relatively weak tornadoes touched down over Russell county, along with up to golfball size hail and 60 mph winds across the area. Additionally, very heavy rainfall produced flash flooding over portions of Russell and Lincoln counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated at 60 mph occurred with the hail.

The same powerful, vertically stacked low pressure system that brought severe thunderstorms to North Central Kansas on Thursday the 14th continued to impact the region into Friday the 15th, featuring very strong northwest winds and seasonably chilly temperatures. The strong winds were driven by a tight surface pressure gradient on the backside of the departing low. At noon, the surface low center was near Kansas City with a strength of 996 millibars, while across far western Kansas surface pressures were analyzed at around 1016 millibars, resulting in a 20-millibar gradient across the state. In between, all of North Central Kansas experienced strong northwest winds through the day and into the evening hours, with frequent sustained speeds to around 40 MPH or higher, and occasional gusts into the 55-60 MPH range. One of the top measured gusts was 61 MPH at the Phillipsburg airport. Also in Phillips County, the high winds peeled a large piece of rubberized coating from the high school track in Logan. These winds made for a very raw mid-April day, when combined with passing light rain showers and afternoon temperatures only topping out in the low to mid-40s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds blew through most of the day, with frequent sustained speeds over 40 mph and occasional gusts near 55 to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Thursday afternoon featured the only severe thunderstorms of the month across portions of North Central Kansas, with reports of hail from penny to half dollar size across several counties. These thunderstorms served as the opening volley to a very active few days of weather, as a classic, powerful, nearly vertically stacked low pressure system organized across western Kansas during the day. By early afternoon, a 998 millibar surface low was centered across south central Kansas, with a warm front extending from west to east across North Central Kansas. In the vicinity of this strengthening warm front, the mesoscale environment featured 500-1000 J/kg MLCAPE, and around 40-50 knots of deep layer vertical wind shear. In response to modest low level heating and destabilization within the northernmost reaches of the mid-level dry slot along with intense forcing from the mid-level low and considerable upper-level jet stream divergence, numerous strong to severe thunderstorms erupted by mid-afternoon, affecting much of the area for around 3 hours before sliding off to the north and east. Hail was the primary severe weather threat with these storms, with the largest reported hail of half dollar size occurring in Jewell and Smith Counties. Hail reportedly covered the ground in a few locations, including near Esbon and Athol. On a positive note, these storms brought parts of North Central Kansas some much-needed rainfall between 1-2 inches, with one of the highest reported amounts of 1.89 inches at Lebanon in Smith County.

Strong low pressure over the western high plains resulted in very strong southerly winds across central and south-central Kansas from the late morning through the afternoon on the 29th. Winds were sustained at around 40 mph at times, with periodic gusts to around 60 mph. Some relatively light damage occurred across the area, ranging from downed trees and tree limbs, to a downed power pole. Unfortunately, one boating fatality occurred in Marion County. Furthermore, the strong winds helped fan a 300 acre wildfire over Reno County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds fueled a 300 acre wildfire during the morning and afternoon on the 29th. The fire started from the rekindling of a controlled burn the day before, affecting locations near Highway 61, between 56th and 69th Avenues. At least 72 homes were notified to voluntarily evacuate. No known injuries or property damage occurred. The Salina Journal and Wichita Eagle contributed to this report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: April 3, 2011, was an abnormally warm day, with high temperatues in the 80s and 90s. Record high temperatures were broken in several locations. A slow moving cold front, combined with a very strong spring storm system, produced widespread damaging thunderstorms, in the late afternoon and evening hours. ||Supercellular thunderstorms, developed along and ahead of the cold front, as it sank southward across the area. These thunderstorms produced extremely large hail, greater than golf balls in many areas, as well as damaging winds of 60 to 80 mph. ||The hardest hit areas, included areas from Leavenworth County south to Linn County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown over just north of Mound City on Highway 7. Thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated up to 60 mph.

A strong upper level disturbance and associated frontal boundary interacted with a moist and unstable air mass to produce a couple rounds of widespread strong to severe thunderstorms across central, south-central and southeast Kansas during the afternoon and evening hours on the 24th. A handful of tornadoes occurred across central Kansas, some of which produced damage and an injury. Straight-line thunderstorm winds of 60 to 80 mph also affected many locations, producing areas of damage. Large hail was not as prevalent, although some stones did reach one inch in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured by ASOS equipment at the airport. Emergency management reported a 6-inch diameter tree limb through a roof in west-central Hutchinson. A 12-inch diameter tree was also reported down. Police reported power lines down in the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance and associated frontal boundary interacted with a moist and unstable air mass to produce a couple rounds of widespread strong to severe thunderstorms across central, south-central and southeast Kansas during the afternoon and evening hours on the 24th. A handful of tornadoes occurred across central Kansas, some of which produced damage and an injury. Straight-line thunderstorm winds of 60 to 80 mph also affected many locations, producing areas of damage. Large hail was not as prevalent, although some stones did reach one inch in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eighteen to twenty-inch diameter trees were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rounds of thunderstorms and heavy rainfall battered portions of central and north-central Kansas late May, causing the Salt Creek, Spillman Creek and Saline River to overflow their banks. Widespread flooding occurred along these creeks, rivers and associated tributaries, resulting in numerous flooded roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The swollen Salt Creek, Spillman Creek and Saline River produced areas of flooding across portions of Lincoln County. Numerous roads were flooded along those creeks and rivers and associated tributaries. Any property damage that may have occurred is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms developed in advance of a cold front and dryline during the late afternoon of Saturday May 21st.  Several of the supercell thunderstorms produced tornadoes including at least 2 brief touchdowns in Topeka.  The strongest tornado was rated an EF3 and hit the town of Reading Kansas.  In all there were 8 tornadoes across Northeast Kansas that evening.  There was one fatality which occurred in the town of Reading where a man was killed when his mobile home was destroyed. There were 5 injuries that required treatment in Reading.  The supercells also produced huge hailstones with some reported to be the size of softballs and numerous reports of baseball size hail in and around Topeka.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief funnel cloud was observed near Emporia prior to tornado touchdown.

An extended period of dry weather continued to worsen across portions of Central and South Central Kansas during the month of May, as some areas saw little or no appreciable amounts of rainfall.  Areas south and west of Wichita dried out even further during the second half of the month, pushing this area into extreme drought (D3). Other areas in Central Kansas, began to creep into the severe drought category (D2).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought (D2), began to worsen across Central Kansas during the first part of the month and lasted the rest of the month. The drought reached extreme levels (D3) across the area during the week of May 17th, with some farmers starting to abandon as much as half of their wheat crop and try and plant other crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and associated frontal boundary sparked a few strong to severe thunderstorms over portions of western and central Kansas during the evening hours on the 29th. The storms produced winds up to 70 mph, resulting in some damage in the Russell area. Additionally, high winds of 60 to 70 mph were generated as the storms collapsed, producing some damage in the Waldo area in Russell county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured by KSN WeatherLab equipment at Ruppenthal Middle School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms were observed in Linn County, Kansas, during the early morning hours of May 23, 2011. There were two reports of hail.

An upper level disturbance over northern Kansas, lifted into Nebraska, during the afternoon and evening hours of May 21, 2011. This disturbance, combined with moisture convergence and diurnal heating, allowed thunderstorms to develop. There were numerous reports of hail and one report of a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1757CST a brief EF0 tornado touchdown was observed 4 miles north of Highland. The tornado traveled about a quarter mile, and lifted a little over 4 miles north northeast of Highland, at 1759CST. Only minor tree damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the very early morning hours of May 24 brief thunderstorms brought some small hail, then in the afternoon a line of thunderstorms moved through portions of central Kansas bringing strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters across Dickinson County reported 60 mph wind gusts near Abilene and Chapman. The AWOS at Abilene Airport also measured a 57 mph wind gust.

An upper level disturbance and associated frontal boundary sparked a few strong to severe thunderstorms over portions of western and central Kansas during the evening hours on the 29th. The storms produced winds up to 70 mph, resulting in some damage in the Russell area. Additionally, high winds of 60 to 70 mph were generated as the storms collapsed, producing some damage in the Waldo area in Russell county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Collapsing thunderstorms produced winds estimated around 60 mph in the Waldo area. The strong winds peeled back and blew away a portion of the senior center roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered severe thunderstorms developed across portions of South Central Kansas during the evening hours of May 11th, 2011.  The multicell storms developed in an unstable airmass ahead of a dry line situated across Southwest Kansas.  Isolated reports of quarter size hail and gusty winds to 60 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator reported the gust.

An extended period of dry weather continued to worsen across portions of Central and South Central Kansas during the month of June,  as some areas saw little or no appreciable rainfall.  Areas south and west of Wichita dried out even further during the second half of the month, pushing this area into extreme drought (D3). Other areas in Central Kansas, continued to be in a severe drought (D2).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3), continued across South Central Kansas during the month of June.  This was a continuation of a drought that started in late April and continued through the month of May and into June. The wheat harvest was worst seen in over 15 years, with some farmers abandoning as much as half of their wheat crop and try and plant other crops.

In all there were 8 tornadoes across Northeast Kansas that evening.  There was one fatality which occurred in the town of Reading where a man was killed when his mobile home was destroyed. There were 5 injuries that required treatment in Reading.  The supercells also produced huge hailstones with some reported to be the size of softballs and numerous reports of baseball size hail in and around Topeka.

An extended period of dry weather continued to worsen across portions of Central and South Central Kansas during the month of June,  as some areas saw little or no appreciable rainfall.  Areas south and west of Wichita dried out even further during the second half of the month, pushing this area into extreme drought (D3). Other areas in Central Kansas, continued to be in a severe drought (D2).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3), continued across South Central Kansas during the month of June.  This was a continuation of a drought that started in late April and continued through the month of May and into June. The wheat harvest was the worst seen in over 15 years, with some farmers abandoning as much as half of their wheat crop and try and plant other crops.

Supercell thunderstorms developed in advance of a cold front and dryline during the late afternoon of Saturday May 21st.  Several of the supercell thunderstorms produced tornadoes including at least 2 brief touchdowns in Topeka.  The strongest tornado was rated an EF3 and hit the town of Reading Kansas.  In all there were 8 tornadoes across Northeast Kansas that evening.  There was one fatality which occurred in the town of Reading where a man was killed when his mobile home was destroyed. There were 5 injuries that required treatment in Reading.  The supercells also produced huge hailstones with some reported to be the size of softballs and numerous reports of baseball size hail in and around Topeka.

An extended period of dry weather continued to worsen across portions of Central and South Central Kansas during the month of May, as some areas saw little or no appreciable amounts of rainfall.  Areas south and west of Wichita dried out even further during the second half of the month, pushing this area into extreme drought (D3). Other areas in Central Kansas, began to creep into the severe drought category (D2).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought (D2) began to worsen across Central Kansas during the first part of the month and lasted the rest of the month of May. The drought reached extreme levels (D3) across the area during the week of May 17th, with some farmers starting to abandon as much as half of their wheat crop and try and plant other crops.

Straight-line thunderstorm winds of 60 to 80 mph also affected many locations, producing areas of damage. Large hail was not as prevalent, although some stones did reach one inch in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs and power lines were downed in Arlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong frontal boundary and associated upper level disturbance sparked numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across the southeastern quarter of Kansas during the afternoon and evening of the 27th. In addition to numerous hail reports and a handful of wind reports across the area, two brief tornadoes touched down in Cowley and Chautauqua counties, but thankfully did not produce damage.

A massive winter storm of historic proportions affected the region from the early morning hours January 31st through the evening hours February 1st. Precipitation started out as freezing drizzle on the 31st, with snow expanding across the area during the evening and overnight hours. The snow, which was moderate to heavy and times, persisted through the 1st, tapering to flurries by evening on the 1st. The highest accumulations occurred generally southeast of the Kansas Turnpike corridor, where accumulations exceeded 6 inches. However, far southeast Kansas saw the highest accumulations, with amounts exceeding one foot. The highest amount received was a little over 18 inches over southeast Labette county. Sustained north to northwest winds of 25 mph, with gusts up to 45 mph, produced near-blizzard conditions at times, along with extensive drifts, some of which exceeded 4 feet tall. Additionally, temperatures in the single digits and teens in concert with the strong winds resulted in wind chill readings as cold as -30 degrees, coldest over central Kansas. The heavy snow, blizzard conditions, and bitter cold wind chills closed numerous schools, businesses and government offices. City and county snow removal crews were overwhelmed by the heavy snow and big drifts. Consequently, travel and commerce were brought to a standstill. At Wichita Mid-Continent Airport, dozens of arrivals and departures were canceled. Where snow was heaviest over far southeast Kansas, National Guard personnel were deployed to assist transporting medical personnel from their homes to area hospitals to perform their essential, life-saving duties. The governor issued an emergency declaration for 53 counties, including most areas along and east of I-135. Numerous vehicle accidents and slide-offs occurred across the area, with a handful of injuries reported. Unfortunately, one fatality occurred on February 2nd in Wichita, when a train struck a car stuck in snow on railroad tracks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of freezing drizzle from the early morning through the noon hour on the 31st produced slick roads countywide. Thereafter, from the evening on the 31st through the early evening on February 1st, areas of snow produced up to 5 inches snow accumulation across the county. Sustained north to northwest winds of 25 mph, with gusts up to 40 mph, produced blowing and drifting snow. Additionally, single digit temperatures in concert with the strong winds resulted in wind chill readings down to around -20 to -25 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy banded snow developed during the pre-dawn hours of Tuesday February 8th and continued through the day and into the overnight hours ending early on February 9th. A narrow band of very heavy snow fell across portions of Ottawa, Dickinson, Morris, Lyon and into Coffey and Anderson Counties where amounts of 6 to 12 inches were common. A few spots reported amounts in excess of 12 inches however those were isolated cases. The snow was accompanied by air temperatures of less than 10 degrees and wind chills of zero to 10 below.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported that Emporia received 8 to 12 inches of snow out of this event.  Other reports from the southern half of the county indicated amounts of 10 to 13 inches.  The northern parts of the county received 5 to 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system gradually developed over the northern Rockies and moved into the central plains on January 9th, 2011.  This system led to a prolonged snowfall event across the region with most areas recieving 4 to 5 inches of snowfall.  Some isolated reports of 6 to 7 inch amounts occurred along Interstate 70 from Ellsworth to Russell, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of snow fell across the region causing numerous accidents across the area.  Sixty five accident or cars in the ditch were reported over a 17 hour period due to eh slick roads.  The heaviest snowfall amounts occurred near Walton and Heston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds behind a cold front moving southeast through eastern Colorado caused winds to gust over 70 MPH during the late morning and afternoon. The high winds blew dirt and sand, which reduced visibilities to a quarter mile at times and caused I-70 to be closed, knocked down power poles and trees, and took the roof off at least one home.  Some of the higher gusts that were reported were 78 MPH at 12 SE of Sharon Springs, 77 MPH at Goodland, 70 MPH at Hoxie, 68 MPH at Weskan, 67 MPH at Bird City, 66 MPH 4 NW of Marienthal, and 64 MPH 4 N of Atwood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power pole was blown down in Kanorado during the afternoon, causing outages throughout the day.  A seven car accident occurred at 1:20 pm MST 3 W of Ruleton due to the low visibilities.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at Goodland.  At 5 W of Goodland a mesonet site reported a peak gust of 73 MPH.  From 1:45 PM MST until 3:30 MST I-70 was closed from Goodland to the Colorado border due to a multi car accident near Ruleton.  At 3 S of Goodland a semi pulling an empty livestock trailer was blown over on K-27.  In Goodland a hundred-year-old tree 18 inch diameter tree was uprooted and a section of roof was blown off a house, landing in the front yard. A gust of 58 mph was reported 12 N of Goodland. After the strongest gust was reported the wind equipment mount was bent over.

The governor issued an emergency declaration for 53 counties, including most areas along and east of I-135. Numerous vehicle accidents and slide-offs occurred across the area, with a handful of injuries reported. Unfortunately, one fatality occurred on February 2nd in Wichita, when a train struck a car stuck in snow on railroad tracks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of freezing drizzle from around midnight through the noon hour on the 31st produced slick roads countywide. Thereafter, from the evening on the 31st through the evening on February 1st, areas of moderate to heavy snow produced 5 to 6 inches snow accumulation in the Cottonwood Falls area, with drifts up to 3.5 feet. Sustained north to northwest winds of 25 mph, with gusts up to 40 mph, produced near-blizzard conditions at times countywide. Additionally, single digit temperatures in concert with the strong winds resulted in wind chill readings down to around -20 to -25 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved northeast from New Mexico into Colorado Friday, December 2nd and into western Kansas Friday night.  The precipitation began as rain across western and northern parts of the DDC CWA, and began to change to snow and patchy freezing drizzle Friday night into Saturday, December 3rd.  The stormed tracked to north central Kansas by Saturday night.  Before it departed, up to 5 inches of snow fell in parts of central Kansas.  The 5-inch maximum of snow fell across northwestern Trego County, mainly west of Wakeeney.  A narrow band (10-20 miles wide) of 3 to 4 inches of snow fell from extreme northwest Ellis County southwestward through central Trego County and then west over northern parts of both Lane and Scott Counties.  Southeast of this heavier snow, there was a 30 to 50 mile wide swath of one to two inches of snow that fell from near Hays in Ellis County to near Ness City in Ness County to northwestern Finney County and then on southwestward to near Syracuse in Hamilton County.  Early in the event, mainly during Friday night, there was patchy freezing drizzle mainly in Ellis, Trego and Scott Counties, which caused a few slick spots on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inches of snow was reported at a location 5 miles NNE of Collyer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged snow event began during the pre-dawn hours of Monday January 10th across Northeast Kansas.  Snow fell during the early morning hours and accumulated in many areas to between 4 and 6 inches.  The snow stopped for a period of several hours during the late morning and early afternoon hours before re-developing by 3pm local time. Another 1 to 3 inches of accumulation then occurred through 11 pm Monday January 10th.  All told there were several counties that did receive 6 to 9 inches of snowfall in a period of 18 to 24 hours.  Those counties have been noted in stormdata.  We have included a snowfall map however it must be noted that 1-3 inches of snow did fall the previous day across the counties bordering Nebraska which has inflated the totals on this map.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jefferson County received anywhere from 6 to 9 inches of snowfall during a 18 to 24 hour period ending just before midnight on January 10th. Reports of of Jefferson County indicated 7 to 9 inches of snow had accumulated during this time frame. The winds were light during the snowfall event and the intensity was not heavy therefore the impacts were mitigated to an extent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A massive winter storm of historic proportions affected the region from the early morning hours January 31st through the evening hours February 1st. Precipitation started out as freezing drizzle on the 31st, with snow expanding across the area during the evening and overnight hours. The snow, which was moderate to heavy and times, persisted through the 1st, tapering to flurries by evening on the 1st. The highest accumulations occurred generally southeast of the Kansas Turnpike corridor, where accumulations exceeded 6 inches. However, far southeast Kansas saw the highest accumulations, with amounts exceeding one foot. The highest amount received was a little over 18 inches over southeast Labette county. Sustained north to northwest winds of 25 mph, with gusts up to 45 mph, produced near-blizzard conditions at times, along with extensive drifts, some of which exceeded 4 feet tall. Additionally, temperatures in the single digits and teens in concert with the strong winds resulted in wind chill readings as cold as -30 degrees, coldest over central Kansas. The heavy snow, blizzard conditions, and bitter cold wind chills closed numerous schools, businesses and government offices. City and county snow removal crews were overwhelmed by the heavy snow and big drifts. Consequently, travel and commerce were brought to a standstill. At Wichita Mid-Continent Airport, dozens of arrivals and departures were canceled. Where snow was heaviest over far southeast Kansas, National Guard personnel were deployed to assist transporting medical personnel from their homes to area hospitals to perform their essential, life-saving duties. The governor issued an emergency declaration for 53 counties, including most areas along and east of I-135. Numerous vehicle accidents and slide-offs occurred across the area, with a handful of injuries reported. Unfortunately, one fatality occurred on February 2nd in Wichita, when a train struck a car stuck in snow on railroad tracks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP Observers reported anywhere from 8 to 12 inches snow accumulation across the county, with 12 to 16 inches over mainly southeast portions of the county. Sustained north to northwest winds of 20 to 25 mph, with gusts up to 40 mph, produced blizzard conditions at times, along with drifts up to 4 feet. Additionally, temperatures in the teens in concert with the strong winds resulted in wind chill readings down to around -15 degrees. The Independence Daily Reporter contributed to this report.

In addition to numerous hail reports and a handful of wind reports across the area, two brief tornadoes touched down in Cowley and Chautauqua counties, but thankfully did not produce damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful spring storm system over the Rocky mountains moved east and strengthened across the High Plains on April 14, 2011.  Thunderstorms rapidly developed by mid-afternoon along a dryline extending from southern Kansas to Texas. With abundant instability and wind shear, the storms became severe and moved rapidly northeast across central and southeast Kansas. Thunderstorms initially developed near the intersection of the dry air behind the dryline and the moisture which surged north from the Gulf of Mexico.  The line of severe thunderstorms produced a few brief tornadoes, several funnel clouds, quarter to tennis ball sized hail, and 70 mph winds during the evening of Thursday, April 14th, 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado briefly touched down in open field.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thin rope like tornado touched down in an open field just to the east of town and moved to the north.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front surged into Central Kansas during the evening hours of Sunday April 3rd, 2011. Strong south winds throughout the day transported unseasonably rich moisture into the eastern half of Kansas as this front moved into Central Kansas. Thunderstorms wasted no time developing once the strong cold front ran into the high low level moisture. Upper level winds greater than 115 mph provided plenty of wind shear for the storms to quickly become severe.  The severe storms brought hail up to hen egg size and 70 mph winds to portions of Northeast and Central Kansas.

Severe storms created a large swath of golf ball to baseball size hail across portions of South Central Kansas on Friday, April 8th 2011. Storms first developed over northwest and north-central Oklahoma along a dryline during the early evening hours of April 8th.  With abundant instability, the storms rapidly became severe as they tracked slowly east-northeast. Many of the large hail producing storms that affected South Central Kansas were storms that split from the large eastward moving supercells over northern Oklahoma.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Baseball to softball size hail pounded the city of Rose Hill.  This hail damaged roofs, siding, cars and homes.  The Rose Hill high school roof was a total loss at $700,000. Damage to city vehicles and buildings were estimated at $313,000. Nine out of 10 of the city police cars have been damaged and one car was totalled.   Nearly every home in town had 50 to 100 percent roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a brief warm spell in late January, winter quickly returned to North Central Kansas on the last day of the month as a large upper level low pressure system carved into the central United States, bringing some of the coldest air of the season with it. On Monday the 31st, the day started out with widespread light freezing drizzle across the area, with slick roads contributing to a single-fatality car accident in Smith County. As the day wore on into the evening, the freezing drizzle gave way to light snow, with accumulations of 1-3 inches by midnight in some locations. However, the bigger story became increasing north winds, with sustained speeds to around 30 MPH and gusts to over 40 MPH resulting in at least minor drifting and visibility restrictions to less than one half mile. In addition, with temperatures plunging toward zero, the strong winds ushered in the coldest wind chill temperatures of the season, with readings plunging to between 25 and 30 degrees below zero by the morning of the 1st. The combination of light snow, strong winds and dangerous wind chills continued into February 1st, when near white-out conditions occurred in some areas mainly along and east of the Highway 281 corridor. By the time snow ended for good on the 1st, storm total accumulations of 1-3 inches were common across much of North Central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall accumulations of 1 to 3 inches, combined with strong winds of 25 to 30 mph and gusts approaching 45 mph at times, resulted in reduced visibilities and hazardous travel across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very potent occluded upper low pressure area moved across portions of Central Kansas into Northeast Kansas during the daytime hours of April 15th, 2011. As this system moved slowly east, a very tight pressure gradient developed on the western side of this system to produce very strong gradient winds across portions of Central and South Central Kansas.  Sustained winds of 40 mph were common across the region during the afternoon hours, with wind gusts as high as 60 to 67 mph reported.  The non thunderstorm winds knocked down trees, flipped trampolines and even toppled a gas station roof.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The strong gradient winds toppled a gas station roof in Hutchinson. There were several reports of tree limbs downed, along with arcing power lines. 2000 people were without power because of the strong winds and arcing power lines.  A portion of a liquor store roof was torn off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong thunderstorms moved over portions of southeastern Kansas during the afternoon hours. They occurred in the wake of significant flooding across the region as low pressure moved towards the region.

Isolated strong thunderstorms moved over portions of southeastern Kansas during the afternoon hours. They occurred in the wake of significant flooding across the region as low pressure moved towards the region.

A storm system moved out of the Rockies and onto the plains, producing a band of heavy snow across portions of northwest Kansas.  The most significant snow fell along a line from Tribune and Leoti, northeast to Hoxie.  Six to eight inches of snow was reported by cooperative observers.  Elsewhere, snowfall amounts of two to four inches were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow was reported five miles south of Tribune.

By the time snow ended for good on the 1st, storm total accumulations of 1-3 inches were common across much of North Central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall accumulations of 1 to 3 inches, combined with strong winds of 25 to 30 mph and gusts approaching 45 mph at times, resulted in reduced visibilities and hazardous travel across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful spring storm system over the Rocky mountains moved east and strengthened across the High Plains on April 14, 2011.  Thunderstorms rapidly developed by mid-afternoon along a dryline extending from southern Kansas to Texas. With abundant instability and wind shear, the storms became severe and moved rapidly northeast across central and southeast Kansas. Thunderstorms initially developed near the intersection of the dry air behind the dryline and the moisture which surged north from the Gulf of Mexico.  The line of severe thunderstorms produced a few brief tornadoes, several funnel clouds, quarter to tennis ball sized hail, and 70 mph winds during the evening of Thursday, April 14th, 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado briefly touched down in a very remote area.  Report via KAKE television.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On late Saturday, January 9th, snow and a little freezing drizzle overspread much of the area.  Snow began falling along the I-70 corridor Trego and Ellis counties late Saturday afternoon, and then spread south through most of the rest of the County Warning Area (CWA) during Saturday night.  By the time the snow ended late Sunday (1/10) evening, 7 to 8 inches of snow had fallen in Trego and Ellis counties.  A 20 to 50-mile wide band of 3 to 5 inches of snow fell from northern Scott county east to northern Rush county, and south to eastern Barber county.  One to three inches of snow fell across a large part of the central, southern and western parts of the CWA.   Far western parts of the CWA received less than 1 inch of snow during the event, roughly west of a line from Syracuse to Johnson City to Elkhart.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 7.5 inches was recorded in Victoria.

Across North Central Kansas, the first widespread heavy snow of the season fell between Saturday, January 8th, and Monday, January 10th, 2011, as an expansive upper level low pressure system centered over the Northern Rockies built increasingly farther into the Central Plains. By event's end, most counties reported snowfall between 5-8 inches, with 9 inches leading the way at Burr Oak in Jewell County. ||This was a prolonged snow event, with many areas experiencing more than one round over a 36 to 48-hour period. While the first round of snow from the 8th into the 9th stayed primarily north of the Kansas-Nebraska state line, things really picked up across North Central Kansas during the mid-afternoon hours on the 9th, and lasting through the overnight hours into the 10th. During this time, a nearly stationary area of light to moderate, and occasionally heavy snow fell across nearly the entire area. By mid-morning on the 10th, light snow or flurries still lingered on, but the vast majority of accumulating snow was over.||Fortunately, winds were rather light during the period of heaviest snowfall from the 9th into the morning hours on the 10th, with limited impact from blowing and drifting. However, northwesterly winds picked up during the day on the 10th, with gusts of 20-30 mph resulting in minor drifting and locally reduced visibilities. Once the snow ended, bitterly cold air became the story for a few days, with most of the area struggling to reach 15 degrees on both the 11th and 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 7 to 9 inches across the county.

Thunderstorms initially developed near the intersection of the dry air behind the dryline and the moisture which surged north from the Gulf of Mexico. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms created a large swath of golf ball to baseball size hail across portions of South Central Kansas on Friday, April 8th 2011. Storms first developed over northwest and north-central Oklahoma along a dryline during the early evening hours of April 8th.  With abundant instability, the storms rapidly became severe as they tracked slowly east-northeast. Many of the large hail producing storms that affected South Central Kansas were storms that split from the large eastward moving supercells over northern Oklahoma.

Across North Central Kansas, the first widespread heavy snow of the season fell between Saturday, January 8th, and Monday, January 10th, 2011, as an expansive upper level low pressure system centered over the Northern Rockies built increasingly farther into the Central Plains. By event's end, most counties reported snowfall between 5-8 inches, with 9 inches leading the way at Burr Oak in Jewell County. ||This was a prolonged snow event, with many areas experiencing more than one round over a 36 to 48-hour period. While the first round of snow from the 8th into the 9th stayed primarily north of the Kansas-Nebraska state line, things really picked up across North Central Kansas during the mid-afternoon hours on the 9th, and lasting through the overnight hours into the 10th. During this time, a nearly stationary area of light to moderate, and occasionally heavy snow fell across nearly the entire area. By mid-morning on the 10th, light snow or flurries still lingered on, but the vast majority of accumulating snow was over.||Fortunately, winds were rather light during the period of heaviest snowfall from the 9th into the morning hours on the 10th, with limited impact from blowing and drifting. However, northwesterly winds picked up during the day on the 10th, with gusts of 20-30 mph resulting in minor drifting and locally reduced visibilities. Once the snow ended, bitterly cold air became the story for a few days, with most of the area struggling to reach 15 degrees on both the 11th and 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 8 to 10 inches across the county.

A strong cold front surged into Central Kansas during the evening hours of Sunday April 3rd, 2011. Strong south winds throughout the day transported unseasonably rich moisture into the eastern half of Kansas as this front moved into Central Kansas. Thunderstorms wasted no time developing once the strong cold front ran into the high low level moisture. Upper level winds greater than 115 mph provided plenty of wind shear for the storms to quickly become severe.  The severe storms brought hail up to hen egg size and 70 mph winds to portions of Northeast and Central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple power poles were knocked down along Highway 196, just north of Highway 254.  The knocked down lines sparked a grass fire along the road.

This Wednesday featured a varied mix of freezing drizzle and fairly light snow accumulations across portions of North Central Kansas, with the steadiest snow falling during the late morning and early afternoon hours before tapering off in the evening. Total snowfall amounts only measured between 1-3 inches in most areas, with the highest amounts favoring places in and near Jewell County, and lowest amounts in and near Rooks County. However, the Rooks and Osborne County area recieved the most concentrated accumulation of freezing drizzle in the morning, with fairly widespread reports of slick roads. Fortunately, winds were not overly strong during this snow event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 1 to 2 inches across the county.  Light freezing rain and freezing drizzle resulted in slick spots on area roads.

A very potent occluded upper low pressure area moved across portions of Central Kansas into Northeast Kansas during the daytime hours of April 15th, 2011. As this system moved slowly east, a very tight pressure gradient developed on the western side of this system to produce very strong gradient winds across portions of Central and South Central Kansas.  Sustained winds of 40 mph were common across the region during the afternoon hours, with wind gusts as high as 60 to 67 mph reported.  The non thunderstorm winds knocked down trees, flipped trampolines and even toppled a gas station roof.
